Flop completely misses QsJc: queen-high with minimal backdoor spade draw. Facing standard ~60% pot cbet from button raiser OOP on a drawy spade-heavy board (two spades, OESD potential with 9T). Claude's range cbets wide (Ax suited, broadway, connectors, weak pairs, draws) but includes strong flush draws/straights. Pot odds ~3.6:1 require ~22% equity, but Q-high realizes poorly OOP and folds out better vs aggression on turn (Claude has 1225 left < pot after call, likely barrels weak or checks strong). Raising polarizes and gets bluffed off; calling bloat pot OOP with weak hand. Standard check-fold per pre-plan vs non-tiny size to control losses and wait for equity spots. Preserve 6000 stack for premium hands.

Ad8c is too weak for UTG, even in a 4-handed game. Early position demands a tight range of premium hands and strong broadway cards. Ace-eight offsuit is not sufficient to warrant playing from UTG—it's easily dominated by stronger hands from other positions. With a moderate stack of 2275 and the need to remain selective with my plays, I should fold and preserve chips for stronger holdings. The large blind (Minni at 22825) and other stack sizes suggest this is a time to be disciplined and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of threes. In a four-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a raise to pick up the blinds or play a pot with position. Claude has already folded. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which has been my standard opening size in previous hands.

T5o is a trash hand with poor connectivity, low card strength, and offsuit nature, offering minimal playability or equity against a button raise. Pot odds are approximately 1.8:1 (450:250), requiring over 35% equity, which this hand lacks against Flash's likely wide button opening range. Minni in BB has a massive 22k stack and acts behind, adding pressure. In SB with a decent 59BB stack early in the tournament (hand 59/150), folding preserves chips for premium spots rather than spewing with marginal equity and reverse implied odds.

Facing a 3bb open from the BTN with 9♥7♥ in the big blind, I’ll defend by calling to 300 total. 9♥7♥ is a suited connector with good postflop playability and strong implied odds with deep stacks (I’m 228bb, BTN ~85bb). Calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives ~31% break-even equity and versus a BTN open range this hand usually has enough equity to proceed. I don’t want to 3-bet a marginal speculative hand out of position — a tiny min-3bet to 500 gains little fold equity and a larger 3-bet bloats the pot OOP without a made hand. Plan: see the flop cheaply, continue on favorable boards (hearts, connected runouts) and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

Check. I completely missed this A♥ K♦ 3♦ flop with 9♥7♥ — no pair, only a backdoor heart flush that needs both turn and river. The BTN’s open-range connects well with this board (lots of Ax, Kx and broadways) so out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal bluff. Checking controls the pot (650 in the middle vs my deep stack ~226bb), lets me fold to sizable aggression, and preserves the option to call a small c-bet to see the turn for my backdoor equity. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve or bluff later; if he bets large I’ll release. For now: check and reevaluate on the turn.

I have flopped bottom set (3s) on an Ace-King-Trey board. This is a monster hand, especially given that an Ace-King-high board is very likely to have connected with Minni's Big Blind defending range. There are also two diamonds on the board, so I need to protect against potential draws while extracting maximum value from top pair or second pair hands. I will place a continuation bet of 400 into the 650 pot, which is a standard size that invites calls from Ax or Kx hands.

Call. I checked this A♥ K♦ 3♦ flop with 9♥7♥ and planned to call a small c-bet to realize backdoor equity rather than inflate the pot OOP. Flash’s bet of 400 into 1050 (~38% of the pot) gives me ~27.6% pot‑odds; my hand has roughly 24% chance to pair by the river plus a small backdoor flush chance, so calling is borderline but correct given the cheap price and deep stacks (I’m ~226bb, BTN ~81bb). Raising OOP with only backdoor outs is too risky — it bloats the pot and commits me with poor equity against Ax/Kx-heavy continuations. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a large raise, and only continue aggressively if I pair a 9/7, pick up a heart, or get a favorable action from BTN.

Check. On A♥–3♦–K♦–Qc with 9♥7♥ I have no made hand and my turn card killed the backdoor-heart plan (I would have needed both turn+river hearts). I only have 4 outs to pair on the river (≈8.7% equity), so I have essentially no value or realistic equity to barre l OOP into a 1,450 pot. Flash opened and c-bet the flop, so his continuing range is heavy in A/K/Q and broadways — poor spot to bluff‑barrel with virtually no fold equity. Plan: check and fold to any standard turn bet; if he checks back I get a free river to try to pair. I would only consider calling an extremely tiny bet (≈≤140 chips) because that is about the break‑even price to chase my 4 outs; otherwise give the pot up.

I have a set of threes on a board of Ah-3d-Kd-Qc. While the Queen of Clubs is a somewhat scary card because it completes a potential straight for Jack-Ten, it also likely improves many of Minni's hands to two-pair (like AQ or KQ) which will continue to pay me off. I must continue to bet for value and to protect against the diamond flush draw. Checking back here would be too passive and allow draws to see the river for free. I will bet 900 into the 1450 pot to extract value from her likely holding of an Ace or two-pair.

Board A♥ 3♦ K♦ Q♣ with my 9♥7♥: I have no pair and my backdoor heart plan was killed on the turn. I only have 4 river outs (two 9s + two 7s) → roughly 4/46 ≈ 8.7% equity. Flash bet 900 into the 1450 pot (pot now 2350); calling 900 requires ~900/2350 ≈ 38% equity. My equity (≈8.7%) is far below the price, so a call is a long-term loser. Raising OOP as a bluff to 1800 is also unattractive — I have very little fold equity vs a BTN c-bet/turn-bet range (Ax/Kx/broadways) and would be risking a large portion of my stack for little fold equity and poor blocker value. Per my earlier plan to fold to a sizable turn bet, I’ll release and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I am UTG with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the stack sizes, particularly Claude's short stack of roughly 21 big blinds, and the general aggressiveness of Minni, there is no reason to enter the pot with this hand. It doesn't play well post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

On the button with UTG folded, 98o is a profitable open-raise from late position due to fold equity against the blinds and excellent post-flop playability with straight potential. Claude is short-stacked at ~21BB and may shove wide, but Minni's massive stack makes her unlikely to defend light from SB. Standard sizing of 3x BB to build pot or take it down preflop. Raising builds my stack through steals in a tournament setting.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with Q9o (Qd9s) — a marginal hand but worth defending against a single raise to 3bb. Raising myself would commit more chips out of position, invite a 3-bet from Claude or Elon, or isolate into a bigger pot OOP. Claude (BB) is short and may still call, creating a multiway pot where Q9o’s equity drops. With a deep stack I can realize equity and keep the pot controlled by calling here. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy aggression if I miss, bet/call when I pick up top pair or strong draws, otherwise avoid bloating the pot.

This is 2-6 offsuit, one of the weakest hands in poker with virtually no equity. While I'm getting decent pot odds (4.5:1 to call 200 into a 900 chip pot), I'm out of position against two opponents who have shown strength (Elon raised, Minni called). With a 2175 chip stack that's still reasonably healthy, there's no value in playing this hand. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities. The weakness of 2-6 combined with positional disadvantage and facing two opponents makes this a clear fold.

I have Q9o and completely missed this dry flop (7s-3d-8c): no pair, no real flush draw and only weak backdoor straight potential. I’m out of position vs Elon, who opened preflop and will c-bet a wide range, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with air. Checking controls the pot and lets me see how he acts — I’ll call a small-to-medium c-bet to realize equity and fold to heavy aggression. If he checks back I can consider a small turn probe or barrel when I pick up a pair or better draw. Action: check to keep the hand manageable and follow my preflop plan to play cautiously out of position.

On the flop of 7s-3d-8c, I have top pair (eights) with a 9 kicker, which is a strong but vulnerable hand on this low, uncoordinated board. I raised preflop from the button with position and initiative, and Minni called from the small blind out of position with a potentially wide defending range. Her check suggests weakness or trapping, but betting extracts value from her worse pairs (like 7x, lower 8x), draws, and air while protecting against her possible overcards or sets. Pot is 700; a bet of 400 (about 55% pot) builds the pot for my strong showdown value, denies equity to draws (like 65, 94 for gutshots), and applies pressure given her position. Checking back risks giving free cards to overcards or floaters. With deep stacks (my ~56BB effective vs her massive stack), this sizing maintains fold equity while getting called by worse.

Call 400. The flop (7s-3d-8c) is dry but I have an open-ended straight draw with Q9 (need a 6 or 10) — 8 outs (~17% to hit on the turn, ~31.5% by the river). The pot is 1100 and the bet is 400, so I’m getting ~26.7% pot odds; that prices a call profitably against a wide button c-betting range. I’m out of position, so raising here would bloat the pot with marginal equity and create difficult decisions; folding would be too conservative given my equity. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, and if I hit the straight or pair up I’ll extract value / switch to aggressive lines as appropriate.

I hold Qd9s on 7s-3d-8c-2s. I have an open‑ended straight draw (need a 6 or T) — 8 clean outs (~17.4% to hit on the river) and a few additional backdoor pair outs. Pot is 1500 and action checked to me. Elon opened preflop and c‑bet the flop but checked the turn, which often indicates a missed or medium strength holding — a good spot to apply pressure. Checking to the river gives me a free card (~17% equity), but betting now as a semi‑bluff wins the pot immediately when he folds and still preserves my equity when called. I want a size that folds out his weaker c‑bet range but doesn’t commit me if called; roughly half the pot is appropriate. Plan: bet 800 now as a semi‑bluff. If he folds I scoop 1500. If he calls, I proceed to the river looking to extract value when I hit the straight and to check/control/fold if I miss.

Facing a donk bet of 800 into 2300 (~35% pot) on a dry board 7s-3d-8c-2s after check-calling my flop c-bet OOP. I have top pair with 9 kicker (98), solid showdown value and blocks some of villain's straight/draw combos. Board remains uncoordinated (no completed straights possible; 2-3-7-8 gaps prevent wheels or broadways easily; added spade draw but I have no spade). Minni's range for check-call flop includes weaker pairs (7x, low 8x), gutshots (e.g., 65s, 94), Ax floaters, and now possibly turned pairs (Ax2, 32) or spade flush draws. Her donk turn is polarized: strong value (sets like 77/88/33 rare given preflop defend, two-pair 73/82 unlikely) or bluffs/thin value. With position, ~4:1 pot odds (2300/800), 52BB effective vs her deep stack, calling extracts thin value from worse while controlling pot size and realizing equity on river. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with her massive stack and induces shoves from better; folding too tight given history shows Minni calls light OOP. Call to pot control, deny further equity realization to draws, and induce bluffs river.

I check. I semi‑bluffed the turn with an OESD and Elon called; the King on the river bricks my draw and leaves me with only Q‑high. Elon’s line (open, flop c‑bet, check turn, call my turn bet) suggests a calling range of single pairs / decent showdown hands and he’ll call a lot of river bluffs, so a river bluff is low EV. With pot = 3100 and him showing willingness to call, I preserve chips and go to showdown for free. Plan if he bets: fold to any meaningful bet (especially pot‑sized or shove); consider a call only to a very tiny probe that offers >10:1).

River board completes with Kh, a relatively blank card that pairs kings or gives top pair to some overcards but doesn't drastically change the board dynamics. I hold top pair eights with nine kicker, strong showdown value blocking some of villain's 8x combos. Minni's line: OOP check-call flop c-bet (weak/medium), turn donk bet (polarized: value like sets/two-pair rare or semi-bluff/draws), then river check (weakens her value range, traps less likely deep). Her range for this line includes weaker pairs (77, mid pairs), missed spades/straights (like 65s, 94s), Ax high. With position, pot 3100, effective stacks deep (my 4450 vs her 20k+), value betting thin ~50% pot maximizes EV by getting called by worse while denying showdown to bluffs/A-high. Checking realizes equity but misses value from her calling range. Half-pot sizing balances protection/fold equity if she has monsters.

Fold. I missed my OESD (Qd9s -> Q‑high) and the river K bricks my draw. Pot is 4,700 and Elon bets 1,600, so a call requires ~25.4% equity (1,600 / 6,300). His line (open, flop c‑bet, check turn, call my turn semi‑bluff, now a river bet) is heavily weighted to made hands (8x/7x/2x, Kx, other pairs) — not enough frequent bluffs to justify calling. Raising out of position into a short stack is too thin and unnecessary. Preserve chips for better spots and fold to this meaningful river bet.

UTG position with J2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. It has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and is easily dominated by better jacks. Early position requires a tight range to avoid difficult postflop situations. With deep stacks (75BB), discipline is key to maximize long-term EV. Folding preserves chips for premium hands.

On the button with A♣3♣ (A3o) against only the blinds, this is a classic steal spot — I have position and a playable ace. Stacking sizes: I'm very deep (~207bb), SB is short (~21bb) and BB is ~95bb. Limping loses initiative and invites multiway action; a raise gets fold equity and often isolates to one opponent. I’ll open to a standard ~3x the big blind (300) — big enough to discourage some light defends and reduce multiway calls, but not so large as to overcommit. Plan: if SB shoves I can call given his short stack; fold to a sizable 3-bet from the BB, and otherwise play postflop in position for value/bluffs.

Raising to 800 total (750 additional) with JK unsuited. This is a premium hand and I'm short-stacked at only 2,125 chips. Against Minni's aggressive button-raising pattern, JK has strong equity. A decisive raise applies pressure, defines hand strength, and gives me the best chance to win the pot immediately or build my stack with a strong holding. Passive calling would leave me vulnerable to blinds and bets. At tournament hand 61/150, I need to accumulate chips when I have strong cards.

I hold AcQc, a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni's raise from the button is standard and likely wide. Claude is very short-stacked (starting the hand with only 1375) and his 3-bet to 800 commits a large portion of his remaining chips, suggesting either a strong hand or desperation. By raising to 2100, I aim to isolate Claude and force Minni to fold her marginal opening hands. If Minni folds, I will likely be heads-up against Claude's short stack with a significant equity advantage. If Minni calls or shoves, my hand is strong enough to continue given the pot odds and the strength of suited Big Slick/Queen combinations in this format.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♥3♣ intending to steal, but Claude (SB, 1375) 3‑bet to 800 and Flash (BB) has now 4‑bet to 2100. To continue I must call 1800 (or raise to at least 3400). The effective 4‑bet size (~21bb) and the players involved make Flash’s range heavy in value (QQ+/AK/AQs), and A3o is poorly positioned and often dominated. Pot is ~3200 so a call requires ~36% equity to break even — A3o has considerably less against a typical 4‑bet range, and multiway complications / the short SB stack further reduce postflop playability. 5‑bet shoving or a small raise isn’t attractive here. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better, more profitable spots on the button.

I'm in the Small Blind with JK (Jack-King unsuited), a premium hand. I've already committed 800 chips with my raise, and Flash has 3-bet to 2,100. My stack is critically short at 1,375 total chips. I need to call 1,300 more, which leaves me with just 75 chips. However, folding here is counterproductive - I'm already deeply invested in the pot (800 chips into a 3,200 pot), and with such a short stack, preservation is a losing strategy. JK has strong equity against Flash's likely 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ, KQ combinations). I'm getting 2.46:1 on my call and need roughly 29% equity, which JK easily exceeds against most hands Flash would 3-bet with. At hand 61/150, I need to accumulate chips with premium holdings, not fold them away. This is a mathematically and strategically sound all-in situation for a short-stacked player with a strong hand.

All-in on the flop with 75 chips remaining and pair of Jacks. With JK on 6s-5s-Jd, I have a solid made hand. No bet facing me (To Call: 0), so I check and allow the hand to progress. At this stack depth with limited chips, I'm essentially committed to showdown regardless. Checking preserves my final 75 chips while maintaining equity with a strong hand.

Claude is effectively all-in with only 75 chips remaining in a 4500-chip pot. I hold Ace-Queen suited, which provides two overcards and strong equity against the range of hands Claude would 3-bet and call a 4-bet with pre-flop. Given the astronomical pot odds and the necessity of realizing my hand's equity, I must bet the remaining 75 chips to put him all-in immediately.

I'm in the small blind with JK and flopped top pair (Jacks) on a 6s-5s-Jd board. I have 75 chips remaining and need to call 75 to stay in the hand, meaning I'm putting in my final chips. The pot is 4,575 chips, giving me roughly 61:1 odds to call my last 75 chips. This is an easy mathematical decision: I have a made hand (top pair with a strong King kicker), and my equity against Flash's likely 3-bet range (premium pairs, AK, AQ, suited Broadway) is substantial. Against typical hands Flash would 3-bet with, JJ is ahead of most combinations. At this stage of the tournament (hand 61/150) with a critically short stack, I must get my chips in with strong holdings. The pot odds are overwhelming and justify the all-in call.

In a 3-handed game, King-five offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button. I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds, or play a pot in position. Given the aggressive nature of both Elon and Minni, I will fold if faced with a significant 3-bet, but opening here is mathematically sound for maintaining table presence and exploiting the button's positional advantage.

Facing a button raise to 300 from Flash in a 3-handed pot. My 87h suited is a speculative hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. As SB, OOP post-flop, but deep stacks (75BB effective) provide implied odds. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 more into 450 (pot becomes 700 if BB folds). Flash's range is wide from button (often raises light based on history). BB Minni may call wide or squeeze, but calling keeps pot multi-way possible or isolates if she folds. Folding too tight; 3-bet risks too much OOP with marginal hand. Plan to call, see flop, and use position dynamics post-flop.

Flash (BTN) raised to 300 and Elon (SB) called, leaving me (BB) with Q♥2♥. Pot is 700 and I must put in 200 more to call (i.e. 300 total committed). The break-even equity on the call is 200/(700+200) ≈ 22.2% — a fairly low threshold. Q2 suited is a weak hand but has reasonable post-flop playability (backdoor hearts, occasional Q). Facing a raise+call (multiway), a 3-bet bluff is too ambitious with this hand because I’d be building a larger pot out of position against ranges that often dominate me. Folding is conservative but gives up good pot odds for a small investment. I’ll call 200 more (300 total) to see a flop, and plan to fold to significant aggression on most dry boards, or continue only with clear equity (top pair/strong draw) or favorable texture.

Flop of Kc 8s 9c hits my 87h perfectly: pair of 8s with an open-ended straight draw (6 or 10 makes the straight). Strong hand with showdown value and equity realization. Pot is 900, I act first OOP in a 3-handed pot. Opponents' ranges (Flash wide button raise, Minni wide BB defense) include overpairs, Kx, draws (clubs flush draw, straight draws like JT, T7, 65, etc.), and weaker pairs Ax. Betting for value against Kx/top pair, protection against draws, and building pot with my equity advantage. Check risks giving free cards to draws or inducing bluffs/check-raises. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (600) balances value/targeting floats/draws without overbetting OOP. Deep stacks support this; plan to continue aggression on favorable turns.

Pot is 1500 and Elon has bet 600, so calling requires ~600/(1500+600) ≈ 28.6% equity. I hold Q♥2♥ on K♣-8♠-9♣ — no pair, no club draw, only backdoor hearts and a chance to pair the queen. Probability to pair a queen by the river is ~12–13% and the backdoor-heart flush by river is ~5% — combined improvement probability is well below the ~28.6% needed. Flash (the preflop raiser) still acts after me, so this is effectively a multiway pot where calling or raising out of position is poor: a raise would be an ambitious bluff into two opponents (including the opener) and likely to be called by better hands. Given my very weak equity and the bet size, folding is the correct, lowest-variance play.

I have top pair with King-Five offsuit. Elon led into me on the flop for 600 into a 900 pot (a 'donk bet'). While top pair is strong, my kicker is very weak, and the board has several draws (clubs, straight possibilities). Raising here risks bloating the pot against better Kings (KQ, KJ, KT) or two-pair/sets that Elon might have called with from the small blind. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, keep Elon's bluffs or weaker draws in his range, and utilize my positional advantage on the turn.

Turn 7d pairs my 7h, giving me two pair (eights and sevens) with a live hearts flush draw. This is a very strong hand heads-up against Flash's button calling range, which includes overpairs, Kx, Ax, clubs flush draws (Kc9c), straight draws (e.g., JT now has straight, 56, T7, 65), and floats. The board is draw-heavy, and betting protects against draws completing while extracting value from top pair Kx, worse two-pair, and stubborn draws/overpairs that can't fold. Checking OOP risks giving a free card to draws or allowing cheap showdowns with worse hands. My flop bet represented strength (e.g., Kx, sets), and continuation maintains that line. Pot is 2100; betting 1400 (~2/3 pot) balances value sizing, denies equity, and leaves room for river action with deep stacks (my 6650). Flash's history shows calling tendencies in spots like this.

I have top pair with King-Five offsuit, but the board is becoming increasingly dangerous. Elon led into me on the flop (a 'donk bet') and has now fired a second, larger barrel on the turn. The 7d is a highly coordinated card that completes several straights (J-10, 6-5) and improves many of his likely calling hands from the small blind to two-pair (K-8, K-9, K-7, 8-9, 7-8). Given that my kicker is very weak and the board is wet with both straight and flush draws, my hand has shifted from a marginal value hand to a pure bluff-catcher. In a 3-handed game, top pair is usually strong, but Elon's specific line of leading the flop and betting the turn into the pre-flop aggressor typically represents significant strength. I will fold now to preserve my stack rather than calling down on a board where I am likely outkicked or facing a made straight.

3-handed play on the button facing only the big blind after small blind folds. Pot is 150 with 100 to call. My hand 9c3d is weak but position is excellent. Standard strategy is to raise a very wide range (40-60% of hands) from the button in unopened pots to apply fold equity and steal blinds/dead money from SB. Opponents have large stacks, but stealing small pots frequently builds stack in tournament. Raise to standard 3x BB size for value and protection.

Fold. 9♠6♦ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand from the small blind facing a button raise to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs 250 more to call, requiring ~36% equity to break even — far more than 9-6o has vs a button raising range (and with the big blind still to act). 3-betting or defending here is unnecessary and would put my deep stack at risk in a poor spot. Preserve chips and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four suited. Elon (Button) has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), Ten-Four suited is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent who has been firing multiple barrels in recent hands. I prefer to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better situation to defend my blind.

Button with 6♣8♠ in a 3-handed spot: marginal but highly playable because of position and very deep stacks (I have ~200bb). Limping is weak; open-raising to seize fold equity and define the pot is optimal. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) — large enough to discourage light callers and keep control of the hand. Post-flop plan: c-bet dry/high-card flops, pot-control or check strong coordinated boards, fold to sizable 3-bets (this hand has poor playability vs heavy action). Raising maximizes EV from steals and position edge while allowing easy fold to strong resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with 92s. This is a very weak hand to defend against a 3x open from Minni on the Button. Playing out of position with such a low-equity hand is generally unprofitable. Furthermore, Elon in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked and could potentially squeeze. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop in BB facing button raise to 300 (to call 200 into 450 pot). Hand 5s Td is offsuit, weak, poor playability OOP. Minni aggressive from button per history, but this hand has low equity vs her range (~30-40% needed but realizes <25% vs typical open). No fold equity worth 3betting marginal hand deepstacked. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Goal to maximize over 150 hands, avoid speculative calls OOP.

I am on the button with Ah8h, a strong suited ace that plays very well in short-handed situations. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage against Elon and Minni.

I am in the small blind with 4s 3h, a very weak offsuit hand with minimal playability. Facing a button raise to 300 from Flash, which is a standard open, calling out of position would be a mistake as I have no initiative, poor post-flop prospects, and no fold equity if I raise. The pot odds (calling 250 into 450) are not sufficient given the hand's equity against a likely wide button range. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Facing a button open to 300 as the big blind with 10♠5♠. This is a suited but speculative hand and I’ll be out of position postflop, but stacks are deep (I have ~20k vs his ~10.7k) so I can realize equity. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives decent pot odds (200 to win the current 450 → break-even ≈30.8%), and against a wide button opening range 10♠5♠ has enough equity to defend. A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to fold enough of his range and a larger 3-bet or shove would be overcommitting with a marginal holding. Plan: call to see the flop, look to continue only with spade/straight/ten/5 connected flops or apply selective aggression; fold to heavy action on dry boards.

Flop: 3♠ 8♠ 5♥; my hand T♠ 5♠ = a pair of fives with a Ten kicker and two spades in hand. I'm out of position vs a wide button opener and stacks are deep, so I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than lead out with a vulnerable low pair. I also have 9 spade outs to make a flush on the turn (~19%) and 2 outs to hit trips, so checking keeps those equity realizations free. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, call small-to-medium bets to realize equity, and fold to large bets/raises that indicate top pair/sets or heavy aggression. If the turn is a spade or a 5 I will become more aggressive to extract value; if the turn is a blank I will often check-call or check down rather than bloating the pot out of position.

On the flop of 3s 8s 5h, I have top pair, top kicker (Ah 8h). This is a strong hand on a relatively coordinated board. Minni has checked to me. To protect my hand against various draws (like flush draws or straight draws) and to extract value from weaker eights, smaller pairs, or ace-high hands that might call, I will place a continuation bet of approximately 60% of the pot.

Board is 3♠ 8♠ 5♥ and I hold T♠5♠: pair of fives with a strong spade draw. Flash's 400 into a 1050 pot is a modest c-bet (pot after his bet = 1450). Calling 400 gives me pot odds of 400 / (1450+400) ≈ 21.6% to break even on this call. I have ~14 clean turn outs (9 spades for a flush, 3 tens for two pair, 2 fives for trips) → 14/47 ≈ 29.8% to improve on the turn and ~51% to improve by the river. Versus a wide button opening/c-bet range many hands I'm ahead of or have good equity against. I'm out of position so I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a check-raise or a shove. Call to see the turn; if I hit a spade/5/10 I will turn aggressive for value, if the turn is a blank I'll often check-call reasonable bets and fold to heavy action that represents sets/top-two. Given stack depths and the favorable pot odds, calling is the +EV line.

Check. On 3♠8♠5♥7♣ holding T♠5♠ I have a pair of fives plus a very strong spade draw (14 clean outs: 9 spades + 3 tens + 2 fives), ~30% to improve on the river. Flash c-bet the flop and now checked the turn — the 7♣ is a blank for my draw and his check signals pot control or weakness. Out of position with deep but not enormous effective stacks, I want to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity rather than bloating the pot where I can be raised off my draw or second pair. Plan: check to the river and reassess — call small/medium river bets with my equity, fold to extreme overbets or check-raises unless I improve to a flush or two-pair/trips, and be ready to extract value aggressively if I complete.

The turn 7c is a somewhat dynamic card as it completes a possible straight for 64 and adds straight potential for hands like 96 or 65. However, I still hold top pair with the best possible kicker. Minni's check-call on the flop and check on the turn suggests she likely has a draw or a marginal made hand. I will bet again to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, and weaker pairs (like a 7 or 5) while the board is still relatively favorable for my holding. I'll bet roughly 60% of the pot.

I hold T♠5♠ on 3♠-8♠-5♥-7♣: a pair of fives plus a 4-to-a-spade flush. Flash's line (button open, flop c‑bet, small turn bet) is wide and his turn sizing is modest. I have 14 clean outs to improve on the river (9 spades + 3 tens + 2 fives) → about 14/47 ≈ 29.8% to improve. Pot odds to call 850 into the 2300 pot require ≈27.0% equity to break even (850 / (2300+850) ≈ 26.98%), so a call is +EV. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise that can be exploited or get 3‑bett off my draw; calling realizes my equity and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in. Plan: call 850 to see the river; if I hit a flush/two‑pair/trips I’ll extract value; if I miss and he barrels small I can call or fold based on sizing, but I’ll fold to extreme overbets that polarize.

Board: 3♠ 8♠ 5♥ 7♣ Q♥ — my hand T♠5♠. I missed the flush on the river and only have a weak pair of fives with a Ten kicker. Flash showed aggression (flop c‑bet, turn bet) and then checked the river, which is consistent with medium strength hands or a trap; betting now has poor fold equity and will mainly get called by better hands while folding out only some overcards. With a marginal showdown hand out of position the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and take it to showdown rather than bluffing into a calling-heavy range. If Flash had bet a small river size I would consider a call versus bluffs, but I will not fire a river bluff here.

The river card is a Queen of Hearts, which is an overcard to my pair of eights. Minni has check-called on every street so far. Her range likely consists of mid-pairs, such as 5x, 7x, or a weaker 8x, or missed draws like the spade flush draw. While I have top pair with an Ace kicker (relative to the eights), the Queen is a scare card that could have hit her range if she was floating with something like QsXs. Betting here doesn't offer much value because missed draws will fold, and most hands that call will likely have me beaten (like a Queen or a slow-played set). Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

Preflop on the button with 62d suited, a speculative hand with flush and straight potential. SB likely folded (no action listed, pot at blinds total 150), BB checked, facing a limp call of 100 into 250 effective pot. Excellent position heads-up vs likely wide BB checking range. Deep stacks allow for strong implied odds if we hit. Folding is too tight in this spot given the loose history and cheap price; raising is not standard with this hand. Limp/call to realize equity postflop.

9♥5♠ is a very weak, poorly connected unsuited hand. With three players already (button called and BB posted) I’d be playing multiway and out of position throughout the hand. Investing another 50 to see a flop with almost no fold equity and a hand that’s frequently dominated is -EV. I avoid spewing chips from the small blind — fold and wait for a better spot or position to open-raise.

I am in the big blind with Kh 3d. Elon, on the button, has limped for 100. Minni folded her small blind. The pot is currently 250 and it costs me nothing to see the flop. King-high is often the best hand or has decent potential in a limped pot, but raising with 3d as a kicker is unnecessary given Elon's limp, which might be a trap or a weak holding. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity without bloating the pot with a marginal hand out of position.

I have King-high with a weak kicker on a board with two broadway cards (Queen and Ten). This board likely connects with many hands Elon might limp with, or at least provides him with drawing equity. Since I have no pair and limited draw potential beyond a backdoor flush, there is no reason to build the pot or bluff here. Checking is the most prudent move to see a turn card for free.

Heads-up vs Flash in BB after my button limp-call and his check-back preflop. Flop 2c Qh Th gives me bottom pair (2d) with a weak 6 kicker, no strong draws but backdoor diamonds. Board is queen-high with straight/flush draw potential. Deep effective stacks (mine ~86bb, his ~125bb), small pot (250). Flash checked flop, representing weakness or traps, but his BB defending range vs limp is wide (Ax, suited connectors, pairs). In position, betting thin for value/protection against his worse pairs (e.g., 22-99, Ax), floats, and gutshots while denying equity to draws/overcards. Checking risks giving free cards to improvers or multiway if turn brings action. Small bet ~60% pot balances as value/protection with my limping range hitting this decently often. Avoids overbetting marginal hand.

In this hand, I am in the big blind with Kh 3d. After checking pre-flop in a limped pot, the flop comes 2c Qh Th. This board is quite coordinated and hits many ranges that include broadway cards. I currently have King-high with no pair and no significant draws, aside from a very weak backdoor heart draw. Elon's bet of 150 into 250 represents about 60% of the pot. Given that I have completely missed this board and my hand has very little equity to improve to anything substantial against a range that is betting into two broadway cards, there is no reason to continue. King-high is not strong enough to call a bet here, and I don't have enough equity or a good enough price to justify chasing. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♢7♣ — a hand with a useful ace-block and good position. With only the blinds to act, raising is standard: it maximizes fold equity, isolates the small blind, and keeps the initiative postflop where A7o has playability. I’ll make a standard 3x-ish raise to 300 total (BB is 100). That size is big enough to fold out weaker holdings but small enough to preserve deep-stack play. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or continue against a single caller and c-bet favorable ace-high or drawy boards, and exercise pot control on dry boards if I miss. If I pair the ace, play for value; otherwise use position to realize equity or take the pot away.

In a 3-handed game, King-three suited (Kd3d) is a strong enough hand to defend the Small Blind against a button open. Minni is the chip leader and has been opening frequently, so her range is wide. While playing out of position is not ideal, the hand has significant equity and playability on many flops. Calling is a standard defensive play here rather than 3-betting, as it keeps the pot manageable against a larger stack while allowing us to see a flop with a suited King.

K4o is a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability out of position in the big blind. Facing a button raise from Minni, who is frequently aggressive preflop, and a flat call from Flash in the small blind, the pot odds (calling 200 into 700) do not justify continuing with such a marginal holding. Postflop, I would be OOP against two players, often dominated by better kings or any pair. Standard discipline is to fold and preserve stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV in the tournament.

On this J-T-2 flop, I have missed completely with my King-three of diamonds, holding only an overcard and a very weak backdoor flush draw. The board texture is quite heavy and likely favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Jacks, Tens, and Broadway draws). Being out of position against the chip leader, there is no reason to lead out or bluff here. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet, as my hand lacks the equity to continue.

I raised preflop and am on the button with A♦7♣. Flop is 2♠ J♣ T♣ — I have no pair but I hold the Ace blocker and one club (two clubs on board) so I have backdoor club potential and decent blockers to strong Ax combos. Flash checked, showing a wide, often-calling range. A standard continuation bet here extracts folds from many turn‑missed hands, charges any single‑club draws, and defines villain’s range. I’ll fire about half the pot to seize initiative and fold out hands like Qx, low pairs, and missed broadways. If raised big I’ll give it up; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look to barreling only on clubs or an Ace/7.

On this J-T-2 board, I have completely missed. I am holding King-high with only a backdoor diamond draw. The board is quite coordinated and connects well with many hands in a button opener's range (like Jacks, Tens, and straight draws). Minni has followed up her pre-flop raise with a half-pot continuation bet. Since I am out of position and have very little equity to continue, I will not waste chips chasing a slim chance of improving. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with 7h5h, which is a solid speculative hand for an open-raise in a three-handed game. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. If I am called, I will have the advantage of position throughout the hand and a hand that can flop well in terms of draws or board coverage.

2s6c is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Poor playability out of position in SB facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). Pot odds ~1.8:1 not sufficient for this hand's low equity vs likely wide Button range. BB still to act, could make it multiway or worse. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in high roller tournament.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have K♦6♠ in the big blind — a marginal holding but with reasonable showdown equity against a wide button range. Pot is 450 and I must put in 200 more; calling 200 into a 450 pot gives ~30.8% breakeven equity, which K6o can reach often enough versus a standard BTN open. With deep stacks (~189bb) I can realize equity postflop, but I’m out of position so I don’t want to 3-bet light (a min-3-bet to 500 is too small to gain fold equity and will force difficult OOP decisions). I’ll call to see a flop and then proceed cautiously: continue with top pair or strong draws, control pot size with middling boards, and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

Out of position with K♦6♠ on T♣‑6♥‑4♥. I have a real but marginal hand (middle pair, good kicker vs worse pairs and many bluffs) and the board has two hearts and a Ten that fit a lot of the button’s opening range. With deep stacks I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a one‑pair hand — raising here would put me in difficult spots against Tx, two pairs and flush draws. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and plan to call reasonable continuation bets while folding to large polarized aggression. If the button checks back I get a free turn to reassess and can extract small value on safe runouts (or protect if I improve).

I have flopped a massive draw with both a flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (7-6-5-4). Against almost any single pair, I am currently a mathematical favorite or have very high equity. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet. This allows me to take the pot down immediately if she has nothing, or build a larger pot for when I potentially hit one of my many outs on the turn or river. A bet of 400 into the 650 pot (approximately 60%) is a standard sizing to apply pressure while keeping the price reasonable for my draw.

Call. I have K♦6♠ — middle pair with a strong-ish kicker on T♣–6♥–4♥. Flash’s c‑bet of 400 into a 1050 pot is small (about 38% of the pot) and gives me ~28% pot‑odds to call (400/(1050+400) ≈ 27.6%). Versus a wide BTN opening/continuation range my pair has reasonable showdown value and blocks some bluffs. Raising out of position with one pair on a two‑heart board is unnecessary and will put me in difficult spots against Tx and heart draws. I’ll call to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn — folding to large, polarized aggression but continuing vs small bets and extracting value when appropriate.

I have K♦6♠ — middle pair on T♣‑6♥‑4♥‑5♠. Flash opened pre and barreled the flop but checked the turn, which looks like a missed draw or pot‑control with a medium made hand. Out of position with just one pair, I want to control the pot and let him reveal more. Betting here would inflate the pot OOP and put me in awkward spots vs Tx and heart draws. I’ll check to preserve showdown value, induce bluffs, and plan to call small/medium turn bets for price (I beat a lot of his bluffs and weaker pairs) while folding to large, polarized aggression. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only thin‑value bet safe runouts (a K or improving 6) — otherwise check to showdown.

On the turn, the 5s has improved my hand to a pair of fives, while I still maintain a massive draw with both an open-ended straight draw and a flush draw. Minni has checked to me again. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking back here is a strong tactical play for pot control. It ensures that I realize my equity and see the river for free without risking a check-raise that might force me to commit more chips than I'd like. If I hit my straight or flush on the river, my hand will be somewhat disguised, potentially allowing me to extract value. If I miss, I have some showdown value with my pair, or I can choose to bluff depending on the final board and her action.

I hold K♦6♠ — only middle pair on 4♥‑T♣‑6♥‑5♠‑8♠. Flash c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, a line consistent with a wide BTN c‑bet range, missed heart draws, or a medium pair exercising pot control. The river 8 improves many hands that beat me (paired 8s, Tx that slow‑played or checks, and any 7x now makes the 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 straight), and I didn’t hit a ‘‘safe’’ thin‑value card (K or another 6). Betting now would largely get called by better hands and only extract thin value from A‑high or missed draws — while also folding out some hands I already beat. Per my turn plan to preserve showdown value with one pair OOP, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and go to showdown rather than inflate the pot on a dangerous river.

I have completed a straight on the river (4-5-6-7-8). The board is 4h-Tc-6h-5s-8s and I hold 7h-5h. While a player holding a 9 or 7-9 would have a higher straight, Minni's check on the river suggests she is likely holding a one-pair hand like a Ten or a smaller pocket pair that she is looking to take to showdown cheaply. I will place a value bet of 850, which is approximately 60% of the pot, to extract maximum value from her likely calling range while remaining wary of a check-raise.

Board: 4♥ T♣ 6♥ 5♠ 8♠. I hold K♦6♠ (middle pair, good kicker). Pre‑river the pot was 1450; Flash bets 850 and the pot after the bet is 2300, so I need ~37% equity (850/2300) to justify a call. Flash’s line (BTN open → flop c‑bet → I call → turn check/check → small‑to‑medium river bet) looks polarised: value from Tx/paired 8x/occasionally trips or two‑pair, and bluffs/missed heart draws or floated overcards. He checked the turn, which makes monstrous value hands less likely to have continued aggressively; it also leaves room for thin value and bluffs on the river. My K6 beats a lot of his bluffs and some Kx/Ax overcards that will barrel as bluffs, but loses to most Tx, 8x and completed straights (7x) or trips. Raising here would only polarize me and be a poor use of a single pair OOP — I have few thin bluffs to balance and would get called by better hands. Given the pot odds, the opponent’s line, and my hand’s showdown value, the +EV, lowest‑variance play is to call the 850 and go to showdown. If he jams over my call I can fold to larger, polarized aggression.

On the button in an unraised pot with 2c3c, a suited connector with good playability. Deep stacks provide implied odds if we connect, and position allows controlling the pot postflop. Raising steals the blinds frequently against the blinds, and we have fold equity. Standard button open-raise size of 3x BB (300) balances stealing and building the pot. Folding is too tight in this spot; calling limps less advantageous without initiative.

I’m in the small blind with KhJs facing a standard 3x button open to 300. KJ off is strong enough to defend but I’ll be out of position postflop and the big blind still has a decision. I’d rather call the 250 more (300 total) than 3-bet: a minimum re-raise is too small to generate fold equity and a larger 3-bet builds a big pot OOP vs an aggressive button. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity (I have good Broadway and K/J pairing potential) and lets me see the flop to decide whether to continue. Plan: call now to 300, evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively if I pair K/J or pick up good draws, otherwise check/fold to heavy resistance.

In Hand #69, I am in the Big Blind holding 10 of Clubs and 7 of Clubs. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The total pot is 700, and I need to call 200 more. This gives me pot odds of 22.2% (200/900). 10-7 suited is a strong enough speculative hand to defend the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, as it has potential to flop straights, flushes, or decent pairs. Given the excellent odds and the fact that I close the action pre-flop, calling to see a flop is the most profitable decision.

I flatted preflop with KhJs to see a flop and hit top pair on 4♥ K♦ 8♥. With two opponents and acting first, I want to extract value from worse Kx, middle pairs (8x/4x) and charge speculative hands while denying free cards. The board has two hearts but no made flush yet; my K♥ is useful but my J kicker is vulnerable to Kx with better kickers or Ace-high hands. A ~2/3-pot bet (~600 into 900) pressures draws/floaters and gets value from worse hands without bloating the pot OOP. If I get one caller I’ll reevaluate on the turn and continue for value or pot-control based on the turn card; a large raise will force a tough decision (likely fold to highly polarized raises). Betting 600 balances protection and value extraction here.

In Hand #69, I am in the Big Blind with 10c 7c. The flop is 4h Kd 8h. Minni, in the Small Blind, leads out for 600 into a pot of 900. I have no made hand and no real draw other than very weak backdoor possibilities. Minni's lead into two opponents, including the pre-flop aggressor (Elon), suggests a strong range, likely a King or a flush draw. With Elon still to act behind me and having no significant equity in the pot, calling 600 is not a winning play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop open with 2c3c from button was standard for playability and steal potential in position with deep stacks. Flop misses completely: no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), weak gutshot straight draw to the 5 (needing runner-runner improvement or specific turns). Board is K-high with two hearts, favoring Minni's range. Her donk-lead of 600 (2/3 pot) after calling pre suggests strength: likely top pair (K), overpair, set, or strong heart draw. Flash folding confirms Minni's credibility. Pot odds ~3:1 (600 to win 2100) but my equity <20% vs her value range. Calling commits ~10% stack poorly OOP postflop; raising has no fold equity as she'd only call/fold weak hands but lead strong. Best play is to fold cleanly, preserve stack for better spots, and realize preflop raise got value/call but continuation ends here.

I’m on the button with 5♠2♦ — one of the worst starting hands. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. Although the button is a natural steal spot, both blinds have shown they’ll defend and sometimes 3‑bet, and calling 100 to play a three‑way pot with 5‑2o gives very poor equity. A half-hearted raise here is thin and easily punished by a defend or a 3‑bet, so this is a clear fold to preserve my deep stack for better spots and stronger holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with QTo. This is a solid hand to open in a 3-handed game, especially when the Button (Minni) has already folded. Elon is in the Big Blind with a stack of 83.5 big blinds, and I have a significant stack advantage. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position if called, as QTo performs well against a typical BB defending range. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Elon.

Ks6s is a strong suited hand with king blocker and good playability from the BB facing a standard SB raise to 3bb. Pot odds are excellent (calling 200 into 400, ~5:1), and postflop I can realize equity well despite acting out of position heads-up. Folding is too tight given the wide SB opening range. Raising has some bluff equity but risks bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand; better to call and see a flop.

On this 10-7-4 rainbow flop, I have top pair with a Queen kicker. This is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind confrontation. The board is relatively dry, although there are some straight draws like 89 or 65. My goal here is to bet for value against Elon's range, which likely includes many mid-pairs, 7x, 4x, and straight draws. A bet of around half the pot is standard to build the pot and protect my equity.

Ks6s is king-high with a spade backdoor draw on a 4h7sTc flop, which is fairly dry but connects well with Flash's small blind opening range (pairs, suited connectors like 87s, T9s, JTs, etc.). However, I have no pair, no strong draw, and minimal showdown value OOP. Flash's 350 bet into 950 (38% pot) is a standard c-bet sizing that gets value from medium strength hands and folds out worse. Calling risks difficult turns/rivers OOP with a hand that rarely improves enough, and raising as a bluff is suboptimal given my wide range and blockers don't block his value hands strongly enough (e.g., blocks KQ/KT but not Tx/sets). Pot odds are ~2.7:1 effective (350 to win 1300), but realized equity postflop is low (~25-35% vs his range). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this 3-handed dynamic where Flash has been aggressive but exploitable.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While I have the best position, this hand is extremely weak. Minni, who is in the big blind, has shown a high frequency of defending and playing aggressively post-flop throughout the tournament. Attempting a steal here against a loose opponent with such a poor hand is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I have very little equity. Given my healthy stack, I will choose the conservative route and fold.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong premium hand heads-up against the big blind after the button folds. From the small blind, we have position post-flop and significant equity against the BB's wide defending range. Folding is too tight, limping is suboptimal as it misses value and allows free flops with weak hands. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is standard sizing to build the pot, apply pressure, and take initiative. Our stack is deep enough at 8100 to handle dynamics, and history shows opponents like Minni defend frequently but we have the edge here.

I have 5♠6♥ in the big blind facing Elon’s small‑blind open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). 5‑6 offsuit is a marginal but playable hand heads‑up — it can make disguised straights/two‑pair and has decent implied odds with deep stacks (Minni 17,975 vs Elon 7,850). Elon has shown frequent preflop aggression and strong postflop betting, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak holding by 3‑betting. The immediate pot odds (about 200 to win ~600 → ~3:1) justify a call to see a flop and realize equity. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed if I hit a pair or a strong draw, and fold to large sustained pressure when I miss.

Flop Qc4c4h gives me the nut flush draw with Ac, plus overcard Jd. Board is paired low, dampening straight draws, but flush draw and possible Qx in villain's range. OOP after c-raise, but strong equity (~35-40% vs calling range) and fold equity make semi-bluff c-bet standard. Protects draw, builds pot, continues initiative vs Minni's wide defending range from history. Size 2/3 pot (400) balances value (if she has Qx/weak) and pressure.

I have 5♠6♥ on Qc-4c-4h and missed — no pair, no club, only a runner-runner straight possibility. Elon barreled 400 into a 1000 pot; calling 400 only gets ~2.5:1 (~28.6%) pot odds but my realistic equity vs his likely range (Qx, pocket pairs, 4x, big pairs) is far lower. I’m out of position and he’s shown aggressive post‑flop tendencies, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a near‑nothing hand or bluff‑raise OOP into a potentially stronger range. Fold and conserve chips for better spots.

3-handed on the button with Js7h offsuit. Position is excellent, pot is 150, calling 100 gives good implied odds if SB limped or folded. J7o is playable in this spot due to position and wide blind defending ranges observed in history (lots of calling stations post-flop). However, limping is weak; better to raise small to steal the pot or take initiative with position. Opponents like Minni and Flash call loosely but fold pre often to raises. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard button steal size, applies pressure, and builds pot if called while maintaining fold equity. Deep stacks allow post-flop playability. Folding is too nitty here for tournament stack maximization.

I'm in the small blind with 6c9h — a weak, offsuit, low-connectivity hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button; the pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call, so I need roughly 36% equity to break even. This hand has poor playability out of position and gets much worse if the big blind calls, making postflop play difficult and costly. I have a large stack and don't need to gamble in marginal spots, so I fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Elon has raised to 300 from the button, a standard opening for him. With a stack of 13,675 and needing to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, I am getting roughly 3.25-to-1 odds. J9o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button raise, as it has decent equity against a wide button opening range. I will call and see the flop out of position.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a relatively dry board of Jack-Seven-Trey. My opponent, Elon, raised from the button, so his range is quite wide. Standard play in the big blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If he bets, I have a clear call, and potentially a raise depending on the sizing, as my hand is currently very strong relative to his likely range.

Flop 7s Jh 3c gives me top two pair (JJ and 77) with Js7h, an extremely strong hand on this dry, rainbow board with minimal draws. Flash checked from BB after calling a button raise pre-flop, which suggests weakness or a marginal hand like an overpair (QQ, KK, AA unlikely given call range), mid pair, or ace-high. No strong draws exist (board uncoordinated, rainbow), so primary goal is to build the pot for value while protecting against any overcards or turn cards that could improve opponents. Position allows control post-flop. Standard play is to bet ~60% pot for value, targeting Flash's calling range. 400 is a good size into 650 pot, offering fold equity against bluffs while extracting from worse (e.g., single pair, ace-high). Checking is too passive and risks giving free cards. Stacks are deep (my 8150), so value betting maximizes EV over tournament.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a dry J-7-3 rainbow board. My opponent, Elon, is betting roughly 60% of the pot (400 into 650). Given the Button vs. Big Blind dynamic, his range is wide, including many air hands and mid-pairs that I currently beat. While my hand is strong, raising here might force out his bluffs or weaker holdings that I want to keep in the pot. By calling, I maintain the pot size and allow him to continue bluffing on future streets or value-betting worse hands. I will check-call this flop and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn 4d, the board texture remains relatively dry and has not changed significantly. I still hold top pair with a nine kicker. After check-calling the flop, the standard play is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donking) here doesn't accomplish much, as I want to allow Elon to continue bluffing with his wide button opening range or betting with hands I currently beat (like mid-pairs or weaker Jacks). Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate his reaction.

Turn board 4d is a brick that doesn't complete any strong draws (no flush, wheel draw now gutshot at best with 5x, but board remains dry and uncoordinated). I have top two pair Js7h on 7s Jh 3c 4d, a monster heads-up with deep effective stacks (~7.7k mine). Flash has checked twice, called flop c-bet into position, suggesting a weak pair (Tx, Ax, maybe 7x or Jx worse kicker), stubborn ace-high, or missed draw pre-turn. Checking gives free card to overcards/improvements and misses value from Flash's calling range. Position allows me to bet for pot control/value extraction while denying equity. Betting 2/3 pot (~1000) builds pot maximally without overbetting, offers fold equity vs bluffs, and gets called by worse (one pair, weaker two pair unlikely). History shows Flash calls loosely post-flop but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hands 47,55,68). Value betting thin maximizes EV in tournament.

On the turn 4d, the board remains very dry. I still hold top pair with a nine kicker. Elon's bet of 1000 into 1450 is a significant sizing, indicating either a strong hand or a polarized bluffing range. Given that Elon opened from the button, his range is wide and contains many hands I currently beat, such as 7x, pocket pairs like 88-TT, and total air. Folding top pair here would be too exploitable. Raising is not ideal because it likely only gets called by hands that have me beat (AJ, KJ, QJ, or sets). Calling allows me to keep his bluffs in and see a river. I will call the 1000 and evaluate on the river.

On this river, the board texture is 7-J-3-4-5. I have top pair with a nine kicker. Having check-called both the flop and the turn, leading out on the river (donking) would likely only be called by better hands (AJ, KJ, QJ, or sets) and would fold out the bluffs that I am currently beating. By checking, I give Elon the opportunity to check back with marginal hands that I beat (like middle pairs or weaker Jacks) or to fire a third barrel with a missed draw or air. Given the action so far, checking is the most effective way to reach a showdown or induce a mistake from a wide button range.

River 5h is a marginal card completing no straight (board 7s Jh 3c 4d 5h has gap at 6, uncoordinated ranks), adds second heart but unlikely flush given flop rainbow with one heart (Jh) and Flash's calling range post-flop. Top two pair (Js7h) remains crushes villain's range: primarily one pair (Jx, 7x, Tx, Ax), weaker two pairs (e.g., 54, 53, 43, J5 unlikely), or bluffs/draws that missed. Flash checked flop/turn/river after calling two value bets (400 flop, 1000 turn), capping range without strong kicker/draw completion. History shows Flash passive post-flop, calls medium bets but folds to larger aggression (e.g., Hands 47,55,68 check-calls then folds). Position + deep stacks (6750 eff) warrant large value bet ~2/3 pot (2300) to extract max EV from worse hands like top pair, while denying equity/setting up river jam if raised (unlikely). Checking misses massive value and risks chop. Bet sizing balances vs stubborn one-pair calls observed.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a board of J-7-3-4-5. Elon raised from the button, making his range extremely wide. He has shown a consistent pattern of high aggression, including triple-barreling with air or marginal hands in previous pots (notably Hand #36). While my kicker is mediocre, my hand is too strong to fold given the pot odds (2.5:1) and Elon's tendency to bluff. Raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me, so calling is the most profitable decision to catch potential bluffs or beat weaker value bets.

I’m on the button with 7♣8♣ suited in a 3-handed spot with deep stacks. This is a very playable, positionally advantaged hand — I want to take the initiative and either pick up the blinds or go heads-up with postflop leverage. A standard open to ~3x the big blind is appropriate: big enough to gain fold equity (Flash often folds to raises) but not so large as to commit me unnecessarily vs a big 3-bet. I’ll raise to 300 total. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; vs a single caller I’ll play postflop aggressively in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Jh 7d. Minni, who has been quite active and often raises from the Button, has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, the ranges are wider, but J7 offsuit is still a weak hand to play out of position against the chip leader. Given the current stack sizes and the need to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding is the most disciplined play here. Calling or raising would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop without a strong starting hand.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB fold. My hand is 6s8d, offsuit gapper with poor connectivity and no high cards. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (about 2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), but 68o has low equity (~30-35% vs wide button range) and terrible playability OOP on deep stacks. Minni raises frequently from late position per history, often with wide range, but defending wide OOP with trash reduces EV long-term. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-hand tournament.

I am on the button with 9d 4s, which is a very weak hand. Given the aggressive nature of both Elon and Minni in previous hands, attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand is risky and likely to be unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips to the pot.

Preflop in SB vs BB heads-up after button fold. Qc3h is offsuit, gapped, weak queen-high hand with poor playability postflop. Minni is aggressive and likely to defend wide or 3-bet. No fold equity worth risking the additional 50 into a pot where I'm dominated often. Deep stacks allow waiting for better spots to maximize EV over 150 hands. Standard fold to preserve stack.

On the button with 3h8h, a suited gapper with good playability and flush/straight potential. Excellent position to realize equity postflop. Facing a small pot (150) and only needing to call 100 into it provides great implied odds if we hit. SB likely limped weak (Minni often aggressive but here passive). BB defends wide. Raising isolates, takes initiative, and applies pressure on limper and BB. Standard button raise sizing: 3x BB to 300. Deep stacks allow postflop play. Folding too tight; calling fine but raise better for fold equity and pot control.

Holding 9♦2♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. This is a very weak, non-blocker hand with poor postflop playability and will be dominated a large portion of the time. Calling 250 to see a flop leaves me out of position against the opener (and potentially multiway if the BB calls), and a tiny 3-bet to 500 is unlikely to fold out enough hands to justify getting involved OOP with garbage. I have a healthy stack and plenty of hands left — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine of spades, which is a very strong suited connector in a three-handed game. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me excellent pot odds of 3.25:1. T9s plays well post-flop and has great equity against a typical Button opening range. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable while out of position.

On this flop of Kh-Tc-2h, I have middle pair with a 9 kicker. While I have improved, the King is a prominent card in the Button's opening range. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most standard and flexible play here. It allows me to see how Elon reacts and keep the pot controlled while out of position. If he bets, I have a relatively easy call to see a turn, as I still beat his bluffs and have some equity to improve to two pair or trips.

Flop is 2h Tc Kh, giving me a strong flush draw (four hearts: Kh, 2h, 8h, 3h) with position acting last. No pair or straight draw, but excellent semi-bluff equity. Flash checked from BB after calling my button raise pre-flop, representing a wide defending range including overpairs, top pair, draws, or floats. Betting accomplishes multiple goals: deny equity to weaker hands, get value from worse (e.g., weaker Kx, T9, gutshots), build pot for when we hit the flush, and apply fold equity against air or marginal hands. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing Flash to rep strength. Half-pot sizing (approx. 325-400) balances protection, value, and fold equity without overbetting into a calling station range. Deep effective stacks (Flash ~9k) provide room for post-flop play. Consistent with aggressive button strategy from pre-flop thoughts.

On a board of Kh-Tc-2h, I have middle pair with a Ten. Elon, acting from the Button, has a wide range that includes many Kings, but also many semi-bluffs like heart draws or straight draws (QJ, AJ) and total air. I am getting 2.625:1 odds to call, which is favorable given my middle pair and potential to improve to two pair or trips. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, and folding is too tight given the strength of my hand against a button continuation bet. Calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot at a manageable size while out of position.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs is a complete brick that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I still hold middle pair (tens) with a 9 kicker. Against Elon, who raised from the button and followed up with a flop continuation bet, I am in a 'way ahead/way behind' situation. If he has a King, I am drawing thin; if he has a draw or a lower pair, I am currently ahead. Leading out now would likely force out his bluffs while I get called by his value hands. By checking, I maintain pot control, allow him to continue bluffing with hands like QJ or heart draws, and can more comfortably evaluate the strength of his range based on his next action.

Turn board 5c adds minimal new draws (gutshot straights like 36, 47) but doesn't complete much for Flash's calling range. I have a powerful nut flush draw (10 outs to nuts) with 3h8h on a heart-heavy board (Kh 2h), plus position and initiative after Flash checks again. Checking risks giving a free card to Flash's floats, weak pairs, or missed draws, while also missing value from worse like weaker Kx, Tx, or stubborn draws. Betting achieves protection, builds pot for flush, extracts value from worse made hands or draws, and applies fold equity against air/marginal holdings in Flash's BB defending range (which called pre and flop bet but passive postflop). Half-pot sizing ~725 balances thin value and semi-bluff; sizing to 800 leaves room for river play with deep stacks. Consistent with preflop button aggression and flop c-bet. High EV spot to fire again.

On a board of Kh-Tc-2h-5c, I hold middle pair with a Ten. Elon, acting from the Button, has a wide range that includes many Kings, but also numerous semi-bluffs such as heart draws, straight draws (QJ), and total air. The 5c on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. Elon's bet of 800 into 1450 gives me pot odds of approximately 2.8:1, meaning I need about 26% equity to justify a call. Given the 3-handed dynamic and Elon's aggressive opening range, folding middle pair here is too exploitable. I am in a 'way ahead/way behind' scenario where I lose to Kings or better Tens but beat all his bluffs. Calling is the most profitable action to maintain pot control and realize my equity.

The river K♠ pairs the top card of the board. My Tens and Kings (T9) is a decent showdown hand, but it doesn't have much value as a bet. If I lead out, Elon is unlikely to call with anything I beat; he will fold his missed draws (like hearts or QJ) and call with a King or a better Ten. Checking is the most strategic play here, as it allows Elon the opportunity to bluff with his missed draws or to check behind with marginal hands, allowing me to reach showdown and realize my equity without risking a raise from a superior hand.

River pairs the kings with Ks, bricking my nut flush draw completely (now 8-high with no pair/straight). Flash has checked three streets, showing passivity, but defended BB wide preflop and called two barrel bets on a draw-heavy board (KhTh2h-5c). His range likely includes showdown value: weaker Kx (KQ, KT, suited), Tx, Ax-high, stubborn draws/missed floats, or even two-pair sets. I have zero showdown value and beat only pure air (unlikely after double call). Bluffing risks: Flash is sticky/calling-stationy (history of light calls like Hand 16 river call, Hand 49 multi-street calls), pot is 3050 with SPR low (~2.7 eff stacks), and paired board reduces his fold equity (strengthens weak pairs/Kx, might call wider fearing chop). Value betting impossible (beats nothing). Checking extracts no value but realizes equity safely, avoids spew, preserves 10k+ stack for 75 remaining hands vs shorter Flash (8k). Consistent aggression was for semi-bluff equity earlier; now pure bluff too thin vs defender. Check behind maximizes EV long-term.

Three-handed, I'm on the button with Q8s (Qd8d) — a playable, suited hand that plays well in position. The table's standard open size has been ~3x the big blind historically, and a min-raise to 200 is too small (it invites too many callers and isolates poorly). I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and keep the pot manageable if I face heavy resistance. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB) is a standard, balanced size here: it steals the blinds often, preserves fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position against one or two opponents. If I face a large 3-bet (one that commits a significant portion of either opponent's stack) I'll fold; I may call a small 3-bet to see a flop because of my flush and Q equity, but I'll avoid bloating the pot out of position against strong aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with Nine-Five of spades. Minni, who has been quite active, raised to 300 from the button. While the hand is suited, it is far too weak to profitably call a 3x raise out of position, especially with a capable player like Elon still to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T5ds is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Defending wide from the BB against a button raise is standard, especially deep-stacked (107BB effective). Button opens wide after SB fold, giving us decent equity OOP. Implied odds are strong with opponent's 175BB stack. Calling to realize equity postflop rather than folding or 3-betting a marginal hand.

Td5d gives us a strong diamond flush draw (9 clean outs to the nut flush draw effectively, since Jd is on board) on a Jd6h3c flop, which is dry but drawy with backdoor straight potential (needing runner-runner 2-4 or 4-7, but mainly flush). Pot is 650, acting first OOP vs button range after defending wide preflop. Button (Minni) will c-bet wide (~60-70% frequency) on this board that misses many of her opening range. Checking allows us to realize equity by check-calling or check-raising value/bluffs later, while denying her equity on missed draws. Donk betting is suboptimal OOP with a draw as it risks folding to raise and builds pot OOP. Check to play post-turn effectively deepstacked (~100BB eff). Plan: check-call small/medium bets, check-raise large bets or if checked behind realize equity.

Heads-up on the button after my open to 300 and Elon called. Flop J♦-6♥-3♣ — Elon checked. I hold Q♦8♦: no pair but Q-high with a backdoor (runner-runner) diamond draw and good positional advantage. The flop is dry and as preflop aggressor I should apply pressure to fold out many of his missed hands (small pairs, weak A/x, Kx, etc.) while keeping the pot manageable. I'll make a standard half-pot continuation bet (≈325). If he check-raises big I'll give up — I don't want to bloat the pot with only Q-high and a remote backdoor draw. If called I can use position to control the turn and re-evaluate.

Td5d gives us a strong flush draw (10 diamond outs left, ~35% equity to river) on a Jd6h3c flop, which connects somewhat with button's wide opening range (Jx, suited connectors, Ax). Pre-flop pot was 650, Minni c-bets 325 (~1/3 pot sizing, standard wide continuation bet OOP). Pot odds to call are excellent: 325 into 975 (~3:1, needing ~25% equity, we have far more). OOP with deep stacks (~100BB eff), check-calling realizes equity without bloating pot prematurely. Check-raising as semi-bluff risks folding better and gets raised/folded to; folding is -EV with massive equity/implied odds vs Minni's 172BB. Plan: call flop, evaluate turn (check-call/call raise if flush hits; fold to large raise if missed and bad board; ready to b/c-r if checked to). Consistent with prior plan to defend wide and realize equity postflop.

Td5d holds a strong diamond flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity to river) on a Jd-6h-3c-2h board, retaining backdoor straight potential and good playability deepstacked (~100BB effective vs Minni's 172BB). The turn 2h adds a second heart, potentially completing opponent draws but also blanking many overpairs/medium strength hands in Minni's button c-bet range after wide preflop open (Jx, broadway, suited connectors, Ax). OOP first to act into 1300 pot post-flop check-call, checking realizes equity by inducing bluffs/value from her wide range while controlling pot size; donk leading risks folding equity to raises and bloats pot OOP with a draw. Her flop c-bet 325 (~1/2 pot) was standard continuation; expect similar frequency on turn. Plan: check-call small/medium bets (~33-75% pot) to realize massive implied odds; check-raise large bets as semi-bluff if range-capped; check-fold only to massive overbets without reads. Consistent with preflop/flop strategy to defend wide OOP and float equity deep.

I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the dry flop (J♦-6♥-3♣); Elon called. The turn (2♥) is a blank and he checked, showing weakness. I only have Q♦8♦ (no pair, no realistic draw anymore), so my best line is to apply pressure in position and try to take the pot now rather than getting to a low-equity showdown. A ~2/3-pot turn bet represents a Jack/strong hand and puts maximum pressure on Ax/Kx and small pairs that called the flop. I will bet-sized to fold out his marginal calls; if he raises heavily I will fold and preserve my stack.

Td5d holds a strong diamond flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity standalone to river, more vs polarized range) plus backdoor straight potential on Jd6h3c2h board. Turn 2h blanks many Jx/overpairs in Minni's wide button range (preflop raise after SB fold, c-bet flop wide), improves some wheel draws (A4,54 suited) but adds backdoor heart for us indirectly. OOP check-call flop consistent with defending wide preflop deep (~100BB eff vs her 164BB). Her turn bet 800 (~60% into 1300) is medium-sized, likely polarized: value (sets like 66,33,J6,J3; two-pair J6+; str8 draws 45) or bluffs (missed Ax,KQ broadway, pure air). Pot odds excellent: 800 to call into 2100 (~28% needed, we have 30-40% vs range + massive implied if flush hits her stack). Raising semi-bluff risks folding better (e.g., Jx) OOP while bloating pot; folding -EV given equity/implied/deep stacks/history of Minni calling down light (e.g., Hands 24,55). Check-call realizes equity, induces bluffs river, sets up hero call if missed or jam if flush. Plan: call, check river (call small/medium, fold huge if missed; bet/jam flush). Consistent preflop/flop/turn strategy to float OOP deep.

Missed diamond flush draw completely; hand is jack-high (Td plays with Jd) with no pair, straight, or flush.
