I’ll call to see the river and re‑evaluate: fold to large river shoves or clear two‑pair/set lines, call small‑to‑medium river bets for thin value or check‑raise/bluff catch if the river is safe.

River pairs the 10s, but my K-high straight (T-J-Q-K) remains very strong, second nuts behind only A-J for Broadway. Minni's turn call of my 150 into a small pot after flop check-check suggests a wide range: medium pairs (QQ-22, Ax), diamond draws that didn't hit perfectly, floats, or weaker made hands. The paired 10s improve some hands (T-x trips, two-pair like K10/Q10) but many of those are still behind. OOP, checking risks getting no value and allows her bluffs or thin value. Value betting extracts from her worse pairs (KQ, KT, QT, etc.), trips that call (underestimating nuts), and any showdown value. Pot 500, stacks deep (me 8650, her 21700), 3/4 pot (375) sizes for value without folding out too much, denies draws/improvements, builds pot for ICM later. Consistent with prior value line on turn.

The river gives me K♥7♠ two pair (Kings and Tens) on K♦ Q♦ 2♣ T♥ T♣. Elon's line (limp preflop, check flop, small turn bet, modest river bet) is wide — he can have missed draws, single pairs (Qx), some Kx, and less frequently a Ten (trips) or K+T (full house). Calling 375 into a 875 pot ~30% pot‑odds is correct: my two‑pair is ahead of a large portion of his range. Raising OOP would bloat the pot and risk getting stacked by the rarer trips/full‑house combos and isn’t necessary to get value from worse hands. Folding would be too tight. I’ll call to get to showdown and avoid putting myself in a bigger, riskier spot.

Ks3h is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by better kings frequently, and low equity against a likely strong range from Minni's preflop raise from the small blind. On the button with deep stacks, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni who frequently raises and has a massive stack, calling risks difficult post-flop spots with minimal fold equity if I raise. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities in this long tournament.

Heads-up from the small blind (I’m on the button post‑flop) with Td4d — a marginal but suited hand that gains a lot of value in heads‑up play because I’ll have position after the flop. Elon folded, so it’s just me vs Flash (BB). Folding the 50 SB is overly tight; completing to 100 is cheap, but I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure with a standard preflop open. Raising isolates the opponent, earns folds, and lets me play postflop in position when called. I’ll size up to a standard 300 total (larger than the min‑raise) to discourage marginal calls and keep ranges wide. If Flash 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold this holding); if he calls I’ll proceed to play aggressively in position.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 6s. Minni, the chip leader and a very active player, has raised to 3x the blind from the Small Blind after the button folded. While the pot odds are 2:1 to call, 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against an aggressive opponent with a massive stack. Given the stage of the tournament and my desire to avoid unnecessary marginal spots against the table leader, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I'm on the button with A♣ T♥ in a 3-handed pot — a very playable, high-card hand with good positional advantage. Standard open sizing at this table has been ~3xBB, so I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I'm likely ahead, and apply fold equity to the blinds. I raise to 300 (3x) to isolate/take down the blinds and retain a comfortable postflop plan in position. If I get a small 3-bet I may call to see the flop; I'll fold or strongly re-evaluate versus a large 3-bet/4-bet since ATo is often dominated by stronger aces.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a three-handed game. Given Minni's aggressive history and the risk of the Big Blind (Elon) shoving behind me, the most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Q2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a button raise from Minni, who often raises wide from that position, calling out of position would be a mistake as it commits chips to a pot where I'll be OOP postflop with poor playability and dominated equity against her likely stronger range. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament where maximizing EV over 150 hands requires discipline with marginal holdings.

I am on the button with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While it's a 3-handed game and I'm in position, this hand has almost no playability post-flop and doesn't justify a steal attempt against a chip leader like Minni or a solid player like Elon. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Preflop in SB vs BB after BTN fold. Unopened pot. Hand 97o has decent playability with straight potential and 9-high can make top pair. Stack depths are deep (81BB), and Minni has been aggressive but in BB may defend wide or fold to steals. Standard SB strategy is to raise wide (~40-50% range) to apply pressure and steal dead money (pot 150). Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a good size for value/protection/steal. Folding too tight misses equity realization and fold equity. Calling/limping poor OOP.

Elon (SB) opened to 300 (3bb) and Flash folded — I'm in the big blind with 6s9d. This is a marginal but defendable hand from the big blind: calling costs me 200 more (300 total committed) against a current pot of 400, so required equity is only ~33%. Elon’s SB open is likely fairly wide, and with deep stacks (my 22.7k vs his 7.9k) I have decent implied odds to hit straights/two-pair. Raising to 500 with 9-6o out of position is thin and would bloat the pot without a clear advantage. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to large, committed C-bets on dry Ace-high boards, float or probe on favorable textures, and value-bet/squeeze only when I connect.

Flop board Ac8d6d is well-ranged for my preflop raising range as SB (includes Ax, suited connectors, broadways). My 97o has a clean open-ended straight draw (hits 5 or T for straight, ~17% equity HU) with backdoor potential, no showdown value but excellent semi-bluff spot OOP. Two diamonds on board make it draw-heavy, warranting protection from overcards/draws and charging them. Minni's BB calling range is wide (offsuit Ax, suited broadways, pairs, connectors) but folds some to aggression postflop per history. Deep stacks (me ~79BB, her 225BB), pot 600; half-pot bet (300) balances value (from weaker Ax/pairs), bluff (this draw), denial of equity. Checking risks giving free cards or facing bet with tough spot OOP; betting applies pressure, builds pot with equity. Standard continuation.

Board is A♣ 8♦ 6♦; I hold 9♦6♠ — a made pair of sixes with a marginal kicker and one diamond. Elon opened from SB and c‑bet 300 on an Ace‑high flop, which is a fairly wide, often continuation‑betting range. Pot is 900 and calling 300 gives ~25% break‑even pot odds to see the turn. Raising OOP here is thin — my pair is vulnerable to any Ace, any 8, or a diamond, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a hand that can be easily outdrawn. Folding is too weak with a made hand. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to big aggression if an Ace or heavy diamond action shows strength; value bet or raise only if I improve to two pair/trips or get clear weakness).

Turn 5h completes my straight (5-6-7-8-9), giving me the nuts as no higher straight is possible (no 10) and wheel requires absent low cards. Board Ac-8d-6d-5h remains draw-heavy (diamond flush draw ~8 outs, some straight draws like 4-7 which we block with 7d). Minni's flop call range (after my SB raise and c-bet) includes Ax (A8s/A6s, weaker AQo), sets (66/88 unlikely), two-pair combos (A8/A6/86), overpairs, diamond draws (AdXd), and straight draws. Deep stacks (me 7575 ~76BB, her 22225 ~222BB) allow multi-street value. Pot 1200; check risks free river card to flush/straights/draws improving, or check-raise unnecessary OOP with nuts. Bet 800 (~2/3 pot) extracts value from her Ax/top pair, two-pair, sets, flush draws/overpairs that call but fold worse on river shove; balances as range bet (weaker Ax, bluffs). Smaller risks underrep; larger (~pot) folds medium strength. Her history shows calling aggression (e.g., multiple big pots calling down). Builds pot for river max value.

Call 800. Pot is 2000 and calling 800 gives ~28.6% break‑even equity (800/(2000+800)). I have a vulnerable but made hand — middle pair (6s) with a 9 kicker. Elon has been opening and barreling from the SB with a relatively short stack, so his range can include many Axs/8x value hands but also bluffs. Raising OOP with a marginal pair is thin: it bloats the pot and can get me committed against hands that currently beat me (A/8/sets) or force a shove from a short stack. I have essentially no realistic flush draw (would need two diamonds on the river), so my best plan is to call to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the river. On the river I’ll fold to clear strength (big bet/shove) unless I improve to two pair or trips; I’ll extract value or call small bets if the board and action allow.

River Ten pairs the board slightly but completes my nut straight (6-7-8-9-T), remaining the nuts as no higher straight is possible without a J or other missing cards. Board Ac-8d-6d-5h-Ts has diamond draw (missed flush), possible straight draws (e.g., 4-7, 7-9, 9-J), but we block 7-9 combos partially with our 7d9h. Minni's range after calling preflop raise, flop c-bet on draw-heavy board, and turn bet on straight-completing 5h includes: top pair Ax (A8s/A6s/AQo/AJo), two-pair (A8/A6/86 rare), sets (unlikely given pre/call sizing), missed diamond draws (AdXd), overpairs, stubborn straight draws. Her history shows calling down aggression in big pots (e.g., Hand 38 called 5000 river, Hand 24 called 800). Pot 2800, my stack 6775 (~2.4x pot shove), her 21425. OOP first to act: checking risks her checking back worse or betting into us awkwardly. Shove maximizes value from her Ax/top pair/two-pair/flush draws/overpairs that call but fold bluffs/draws; smaller bet risks underrep or multi-bet river. Perfect spot to get stack in with nuts vs calling station tendencies.

Pot is 9,575 and Elon jammed for 6,775; calling 6,775 to win 16,350 requires ~41.5% equity (6,775 / (9,575+6,775) ≈ 0.415). I hold 9♦6♠ — only a single pair of sixes (best five: 6s6♦A♣T♠9♦). Elon opened from the SB, c-bet the A‑high flop, barreled the turn and now shoves his short stack on the river. That line is heavily weighted toward value: A‑x, 8‑x, hands that make two pair (T6, T5, 86), sets/boats, and straight combos (7‑9, 4‑7) — all of which beat my pair. He can have some missed diamond semi‑bluffs, and my 9 removes a few 7‑9 combos, but not nearly enough to push his bluff frequency over the ~41.5% threshold I need.

Calling would commit ~32% of my stack for a vulnerable one‑pair hand that is frequently dominated. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

7s2c is one of the worst possible hands in poker: offsuit, non-connected, low cards with poor playability. Even on the button with position, the EV of calling 100 into 150 is negative due to dominated ranges and poor post-flop potential. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Opponents' stacks don't change this; Minni is deep, Flash medium, but no need to speculate with trash.

6♣3♦ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor playability out of position in the small blind. Calling 50 to play OOP against Flash (who has shown aggressive, defending tendencies) gives marginal pot odds (~25%) but my hand’s equity and post‑flop playability are well below what I need. Raising is irresponsible — it’s a bluff with almost no fold equity and would invite 3-bets. Preserve my stack for better spots and fold.

On the button with Qc7s (offsuit queen-seven) in a 3-handed pot I want to leverage position and take the initiative. Limping/calling 100 surrenders the initiative and invites the blinds to see a cheap flop; a small min-raise (200) is too weak and will attract more calls/3-bets. With deep stacks and opponents who fold to position pressure often, a standard 3x open to 300 maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: open to 300; if faced with a large 3-bet I will generally fold Q7o out of position, but I can defend or call small 3-bets to use positional advantage postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the Button. While Minni has been quite active, T7o is a weak hand to defend out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against a larger stack, and raising as a bluff is risky given Minni's tendency to call or put on pressure. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

AQo is a premium hand from the BB facing a button raise. Minni is frequently aggressive and raises wide from late position based on hand history (e.g., many steals and light opens). This range includes many hands I dominate like KQo, AJo, suited broadways, pairs below QQ. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot with my equity advantage, and takes initiative on a deep stack. Calling risks multiway pots if Flash wakes up, but he's folded. Folding is -EV. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard for value and pressure without overcommitting.

Elon 3-bet to 900 after my 300 open. I have Q♣7♠ on the button in a 3-handed pot — position is a big plus. The 3-bet size is standard/small; calling costs 600 into a 1,250 pot so I need roughly 32% equity to break even. Q7o is marginal vs a very tight polarized 3-bet range, but vs a wider/standard 3-bet range and with position I can realize equity and outplay him postflop. 4-betting as a bluff (min to 1,500) commits too much with a dominated hand and folding surrenders too much of the pot. I’ll call to 900 total (600 more) to see a flop and then: continue aggressively on favorable boards (Q/7 or coordinated/drawy flops), use positional pressure with c-bets and delayed bluffs, and check/fold to heavy aggression on dry, clearly dominated boards.

Heads-up on the flop with AsQd on 4s6d8s. I have the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity to flush) plus the Ace overcard, top pair blocker, and strong 3-bet initiative. This board hits my 3-bet range well (Ax suited, sets, etc.) while Minni's calling range (pairs TT+, broadway, suited connectors) often has overpairs, mid pairs, or weaker draws. Betting builds the pot with my massive equity (~45%+ vs her range), charges her draws/pairs, denies equity to floats, and protects against runner-runner straights. Checking risks giving free cards to her overcards or backdoor draws. Sizing to 1200 (~65% pot) balances value from pairs while keeping stacks playable (leaves ~7500 behind). Her deep stack and button position suggest she'll continue with some equity, but fold weak hands.

I called Elon's small preflop 3-bet to play the hand in position. Flop 4s 6d 8s and Elon bets 1,200 into a 3,050 pot. With Qc7s I have a gutshot to a 5 (4 outs) plus 3 queens and 3 sevens — roughly 10 clean outs (~38% to improve by the river). Calling 1,200 into a ~4,250 effective pot (~28% break-even equity) is +EV. I keep position, realize my equity, and can float/bluff-turn or extract value if I pair or hit the straight. A raise here is too thin versus a 3-bettor and folding concedes too much. Plan: call the 1,200 and re-evaluate on the turn — bet/raise for value if I improve, fold to clear strength if I miss and face heavy aggression.

Heads-up on turn with AsQd on 4s6d8s3c board. Still holding the nut spade flush draw (9 outs ~19% to hit river), Ace overcard blocker, and strong initiative from 3-bet pre and flop c-bet. The 3c is a relatively blank card: low, clubs don't complete a strong flush, and it brings minor gutshots (e.g., 5 for 3-4-5-6, but weak). Minni's range for calling 3-bet and flop bet on spade-heavy board likely includes: weaker flush draws (like KsJs, 9s7s, suited connectors), pair + draw (88/66/44/set mines unlikely), overpairs (JJ+, KK), or combo draws. My equity remains high (~40%+ vs her range) due to draw + initiative. With low SPR (~1.75:1, my 7475 into 4250), continue aggression to deny equity, charge her draws/improvers, extract value from pairs/top of range, and set up river shove if checked to. Checking risks free river cards (spade, runner-runner straight/fill), especially since she has position. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (2800) balances value/protection, keeps pot manageable, and applies max pressure without overcommitting (leaves ~4700 behind for river). Her deep stack (19275) allows her to continue light, but history shows she folds to sustained pressure when behind (e.g., Hands 39,55,60). This maximizes EV in tournament spot.

Elon’s turn bet of 2,800 into a 7,050 pot requires roughly 28% equity to make a break-even call (2800 / (7050+2800) ≈ 28.4%). After the turn (4♠ 6♦ 8♠ 3♣) my Q♣7♠ has only one card left to improve. Raw river outs: 3 queens + 3 sevens + 4 fives = 10 outs → 10/46 ≈ 21.7% — already below the required 28%. More importantly, many of those pair-outs (Q/7) are not “clean” against a player who 3‑bet preflop and has continued firing (A‑x and big pairs dominate those). The only really clean outs versus a strong continuing range are the four fives (making the 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 straight) (~8.7%). Raising as a bluff here is too thin — Elon has shown consistent strength and a raise to 5,600+ would either fold out only marginal hands or commit me into a spot where my equity is poor. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better, more +EV opportunities.

I am on the button with Queen-Five suited. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to take the pot pre-flop. Both Elon and Minni have been active, but playing in position is a significant advantage. I will raise to 300 to see where I stand; if I'm re-raised, I can comfortably fold, but if called, I have a hand with some equity and positional control.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise. Flash's button raise could be wide, but calling OOP gives poor implied odds and position disadvantage against a likely continuing range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no value in calling or raising this marginal king.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and Elon folded, so it's heads-up. I have KdQc in the big blind and deep stacks (19,175 vs 8,700). KQo is a clear defend — it has good equity vs a wide opening range and plays well postflop. A min-raise to 500 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity (and I'd rather 3-bet larger if I wanted to take the initiative), so I prefer to call 200 more and keep the pot under control out of position. Plan: see the flop and play aggressively if I hit top pair or a strong draw; be prepared to fold to large sustained aggression on A-high or very dry boards.

Flop is 6s-6c-3d and I hold Kd-Qc — two overcards but no pair. Flash opened preflop so his range contains a lot of pocket pairs and some connected Broadway hands; on this paired, dry, low board I’m behind most 6x/3x and medium pairs but still have ~reasonable showdown equity vs a wide c-bet range. I’m out of position and stacks are deep (high SPR), so I don’t want to build a big pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and control the pot, call small c-bets to realize equity (≈≤1/3 pot) but fold to large sustained aggression unless I improve. If he checks back I can take a free turn card and re-evaluate. Therefore I’ll check to keep options open and avoid bloating the pot OOP with KQ.

The board of 6s-6c-3d is very dry and typically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since I raised from the button and Minni called from the big blind, her range is likely capped. A small continuation bet here is highly effective for taking down the pot immediately against hands that missed the flop. I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw, so if called, I still have some potential to improve or barrel later. I'll bet 225 into the 650 pot.

Flash opened and I defended KdQc from the big blind. Flop 6s-6c-3d is dry and my hand is two overcards with decent showdown equity vs a wide c-bet but behind most 6x/3x and any pocket pair. His bet 225 into 875 is small (~25% pot). With deep effective stacks (≈8,475) and SPR ~7–8 I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with just overcards. Calling realizes equity, keeps the pot controlled, and preserves options if I hit a K/Q or get a good turn. I will call the 225 and re-evaluate on the turn, folding to large sustained aggression unless I improve.

Board: 6s-6c-3d-7h. I hold Kd-Qc — just two overcards, no pair. I called the tiny flop c-bet to realize equity; the turn 7h doesn't improve me (6 outs to pair ~13%). Opponent checked to me, which can be weakness or pot control, but with deep effective stacks (≈8.5k) and SPR ≈7–8 I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Betting here would be a thin bluff that can be check‑raised or called by any 6x/7x/pair that already beats me. I choose the low-variance line: check and see the river. If I pair the river I can bet for value; if I miss and face significant aggression I'll fold.

On this paired and dry board (6s 6c 3d 7h), Minni's call on the flop suggests she either has a small pocket pair (like 44 or 55), a piece of the board (a 3 or a 6), or an Ace-high float. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but the turn 7h doesn't significantly change the board texture. While a second barrel could potentially force out her weaker floats or tiny pairs, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, see a free river card, and keep the pot manageable. If the river is a Queen or a Five, I might have the best hand; if not, I can decide whether to bluff or give up based on her river action.

Board: 6s 6c 3d 7h Ah. I hold KdQc — no pair, missed completely. I called a small flop c-bet and both checked the turn to keep the pot small; the Ace on the river drastically reduces my equity (many hands in Flash’s open/c-bet range — Ax, 6x, medium pairs — now beat me). Betting as a bluff here would be thin: Flash checked the turn and is likely to call a river stab with any pair or Ax, so my fold equity is low and I’d be building a pot out of position with essentially no showdown value. Low-variance, correct line is to check and preserve my stack; if Flash bets I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression, if he checks back I show down and can occasionally win vs missed broadways.

The river Ace is an excellent scare card to bluff. After raising pre-flop and betting the flop, I checked back the turn to control the pot. Minni's check on the river indicates she likely doesn't have a 6 or a strong Ace. My Queen-high is unlikely to win at showdown against her range of small pocket pairs (22, 44, 55) or a 3. By betting approximately 65% of the pot, I am representing an Ace that checked the turn for pot control. This line is very credible for a button open, and it should force Minni to fold most of her marginal showdown hands.

I hold K♦Q♣ and missed — the river A♥ knocks out almost all the hands I can realistically beat. Flash’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, check turn, small bet on the A river) is consistent with a lot of Ax, 6x and 7x value hands that now beat me; there are only a few pure bluffs I beat. Calling 700 into a 1,800 pot requires ≈28% equity; my hand’s equity versus a river‑betting range that contains many Ax/sets/pairs is well below that. Raising would be a very thin bluff — the min‑raise to 1,400 is unlikely to fold enough value hands and would needlessly bloat the pot out of position. As the chip leader I’ll avoid marginal, high‑variance spots: fold and preserve my stack.

43o is an extremely weak offsuit unconnected hand with no high card value or playability. Even on the button with deep stacks, it has terrible equity against any reasonable calling or raising range from SB or BB. Facing a to-call of 100 into 150 pot offers insufficient odds (needing ~40% equity which this hand lacks multiway or heads-up vs typical ranges). Standard fold to preserve stack for premium opportunities in this high roller tournament.

I'm in the small blind with T♦4♠, heads-up against the big blind (Flash). This is a weak hand to see a flop out of position, but heads-up SB vs BB allows a wide open-raise to steal the blind. I want to take the initiative and avoid limp-play OOP where I'm likely awkward postflop. I'll make a minimum raise to 200 to apply pressure — if Flash folds I pick up the 150 chip pot, if he calls I proceed cautiously OOP (small c-bets on favorable flops, pot control on bad boards). If he 3-bets large I'll fold this weak holding. Stack depth (18.7k) lets me be flexible and fold to significant aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The button folded and the Small Blind, Minni, has raised to 200. I only need to put in 100 more to call into a 300 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds (or 4:1 depending on the calculation of the final pot). Given that I will be in position for the rest of the hand against Minni, and the price is very cheap, calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I raised preflop to take the initiative heads-up; on a dry K♦7♦2♣ (rainbow) flop my T♦4♠ has very little showdown value and no real redraws. Heads-up vs a caller’s likely wide range I can take this down with a standard continuation bet — the board is dry enough that a half-pot c-bet will fold many single-card hands and give me info without bloating the pot out of position. I’ll make a conservative c-bet of 200 (≈½ pot). If Flash folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll check the turn to control the size of the pot and re-evaluate on the next card. If he check-raises or shows strong aggression I’ll fold this hand rather than committing more with air.

On a flop of K-7-2, I have middle pair (Sevens) with a Jack kicker. Minni leads for half the pot (200 into 400) after raising pre-flop. This is a standard continuation bet on a dry board. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call and I have position for the remainder of the hand. While a King is certainly in Minni's range, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, as I beat all of her bluffs and some weaker pairs. I will call to see the turn and evaluate based on her next action.

I have T♦4♠ — essentially air. I c-bet the dry K-7-2 flop to fold worse hands, but Flash’s call narrows his range to many hands that beat me (Kx, 7x, small pairs, occasionally a 2). The turn pairing the board with a 2 helps his range more (possible trips/2x) and makes a successful bluff much less likely. I’m out of position and the pot is already 800; there’s no upside to building the pot with nothing. Check and concede to any meaningful bet — control the pot size now and preserve stack for better spots. If he wakes up with a harmless float and checks back I can reassess on the river, but I will fold to pressure.

On this board of K-7-2-2, I have a pair of Sevens. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, has now checked the turn. My hand has significant showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value since I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I risk getting check-raised by a King or a slow-played Deuce. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and reach a showdown more reliably.

The river 4♥ gives me a weak pair of fours, but it’s still very vulnerable. Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn after the board paired with a 2 — that line is consistent with Kx, 7x, medium pocket pairs or cautious floats; many of those hands beat my lone 4. Betting now will rarely get value (only from missed overcards or rare bluffs) and risks bloating the pot OOP or getting raised by better hands. The pot is small (800) relative to stacks, so the lowest-variance line is to check and take showdown. If Flash bets a small thin value/bluff I’ll consider a call; I’ll fold to a large river raise.

On the river with a board of K-7-2-2-4, I hold a pair of Sevens. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor who led the flop, has checked both the turn and the river. My hand has significant showdown value against her range, which includes many Ace-high hands or smaller pairs that she might have given up on. Betting for value is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and checking back ensures I reach showdown with a hand that is very likely to be the winner without risking a check-raise.

3-handed on the button with K8o — a clear open-raise spot. Blinds are in (pot=150) and to call is 100, min-raise 200. I want to seize the initiative and exploit position, so I’ll make a standard 3x-style open to 300: big enough to apply pressure to the blinds and consistent with the table sizing we’ve seen. Stacks are deep so no need to shove; raising preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (K8o plays poorly vs heavy aggression), but if called use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry/King-high boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 2h, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the button to 300, and although the pot is laying me odds to call, playing out of position with a garbage hand against the table chip leader is a losing strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

7s3s is a weak gapped suited hand with low playability out of position versus a button raise. Minni's range is wide but still favors folding trash like this. Pot odds are 450:200 (2.25:1), but equity is insufficient (~30% vs loose range), and postflop OOP is bad. Preserve stack for better spots.

In a 3-handed game, hand ranges are significantly wider. From the button, J7o is a borderline opening hand, but it falls within a standard 3-handed opening range (typically the top 50% of hands). Given the current chip stacks and the need to maintain pressure, I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play the pot with positional advantage.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). Hand is Tc5d, which is a complete trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, dominated by any calling range, and poor playability out of position postflop. Effective stacks are deep (mine 115x BB, opponents similar), so no need to speculate with marginal equity. Button raisers (Flash) can be wide, but folding weak hands OOP maximizes EV long-term in this tournament format. History shows no exploitable weakness justifying a call or 3bet. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I have 7♥3♠ — a very weak, poorly coordinated offsuit hand. Flash opened to 300 from the button; calling 200 into a 450 pot gives ~24% equity required, but out of position against a button opener I’ll be dominated by most of his raising range and face difficult multi-street decisions with very little chance to realize equity. 3-betting as a bluff is possible but 7-3o has terrible blockers and is a poor candidate for an isolated bluff against a capable opponent. With deep stacks and 3 players left I’d rather preserve my stack and wait for a better spot in position or with a hand that plays well postflop. Fold and defend better spots later.

Pocket tens (Tc Ts) is a premium hand 3-handed, especially on the button with position on both opponents. No pre-flop action yet (standard blinds posted, pot 150, to call BB 100). Standard play is to raise for value to build the pot, thin the field, and take initiative. Minni (SB) and Flash (BB) have shown tendencies to call raises wide or defend, but TT has great equity. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a balanced size that gets value from worse hands while not overcommitting deep stacks. My stack 11525 allows flexibility post-flop.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the small blind with A♠3♣ (As 3c). Stacks are deep (I have 18,350), but A3o is a marginal, often‑dominated hand and I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with little fold equity. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity and see a flop (and I can fold to heavy action if it goes multiway and gets ugly). I’ll call the 250 more (total committed this street = 300 including my 50 SB) and re-evaluate on the flop.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ac Ah), the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon opened from the button for 300, and Minni called from the small blind. In a three-way pot, I want to build the pot for value and narrow the field to protect my equity. A standard 3-bet size here, considering there is already a caller, would be around 3.5x to 4x the opening raise plus the caller's amount. Raising to 1,100 total puts significant pressure on both opponents and sets up a favorable stack-to-pot ratio for post-flop play.

Pocket Tens is still a very strong hand 3-handed on the button with position. Flash's 3-bet from the BB after my open and Minni's call is polarized: either premium hands like QQ+/AK or bluffs/light 3-bets given the history of loose play from opponents. Folding TT here is too tight preflop as it has excellent equity against calling/3-betting ranges (~35-45% vs typical 3-bet range). 4-betting risks bloating the pot OOP multiway or getting isolated by Minni who calls wide. Calling keeps the pot manageable (pot odds ~3:1 on the 800 to call into 2500 effective pot), realizes equity well multiway, and maintains flexibility postflop with position on both. My deep stack (11225) allows this; we can play aggressively on favorable boards. History shows opponents check/fold or call lightly postflop often.

I initially called to keep the pot small, but Flash’s sizable 3‑bet to 1100 and Elon’s call put me multiway and out of position. A♠3♣ is frequently dominated by both players’ calling/3‑bet ranges (Ax, broadways, pairs). Calling another 800 (pot ~2500 → need ~24% equity) is priced poorly multiway and a raise would be committing too many chips OOP with a weak kicker and little fold equity. Preserve my deep stack and fold here.

I hold pocket Aces on a board that is somewhat coordinated (Td-6d-8h) with flush and straight possibilities. Since I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage. I want to bet for value against 10x hands, JJ, and various draws. A bet of 1,000 chips (40% of the pot) allows me to extract value and protect my equity while keeping the stack-to-pot ratio manageable for later streets.

Flopped top set with TT on Td6d8h board heads-up IP against Flash who 3-bet preflop from BB and now donk bets 1000 into 2500-ish pot. This is the nuts or near-nuts; board has diamond flush draw and possible straight draws (97s, 75, 54), so raising protects against draws while extracting value from his overpairs (QQ/JJ), two-pair (T8, T6, 86), or worse sets (66). His donk bet after 3-bet suggests either strong value, a draw, or a bluff to deny equity. Raising builds the pot with our massive equity (~90%+ vs calling range) and deep effective stacks (his 7975). Size to ~3x his bet or pot-committing relative to his stack: raise to 3000 total, offering good fold equity vs bluffs/draws while getting calls from 88/66/Tx. Position allows control postflop if he shoves/continues.

I hold pocket Aces, the strongest possible overpair, on a coordinated board with both flush and straight draws. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor and led the flop for 1,000, and Elon's raise to 3,000 signals either a very strong made hand (like a set or straight) or a powerful draw. Given the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR), I am effectively committed to this hand. With the pot currently at 6,500 and Elon having only 4,425 left in his stack, an all-in raise is the most strategic move. This maximizes value from his strong draws (diamond flushes, straight draws) and hands like JJ, QQ, or any Ten, while protecting my equity against the many cards that could roll off on the turn to kill the action or beat me. Elon's history of aggression suggests he is capable of making this move with a wide range of hands, making the shove the best play for both value and protection.

Top set with TT on Td6d8h flop is an extremely strong hand, nearly the nuts, crushing overpairs, two-pair combos, weaker sets (66/88), flush draws (AdXd), and straight draws (79s, 75s, 54s). Flash's line (BB 3-bet pre, flop donk 1000 into 2450 pot, then 4-bet jam to 7975 over my raise to 3000) is polarized: very strong value (88, T8/T6/86 unlikely given board), draws, or bluffs. My equity is 80-90%+ against his range given history of loose aggressive play and frequent bluffs/showdowns. Pot odds are ~2.7:1 (4975 to call into 13475, but effective >3:1 considering his 1000 behind), far better than required. Position allows control on turn/river. Can't raise (stack 7425 < min raise 8975). Folding premium monster is -EV. Call realizes equity perfectly, builds massive pot where I'm huge favorite, and leaves me with 2450 behind his 1000 for easy calls later streets if needed. Goal to maximize stack long-term favors stacking off with this strength 3-handed.

I have turned a set of Aces (AAA), which is an incredibly strong hand. The board now shows three diamonds (Td 6d Ad), making a flush possible for my opponent. However, with only 1,000 chips remaining and a pot of 18,450, the stack-to-pot ratio is extremely low (0.05), making me mathematically committed to the hand. Even if Elon has already completed a flush, I still have 10 outs (three Tens, three Eights, three Sixes, and the case Ace) to improve to a full house or four-of-a-kind on the river. I will bet my remaining stack to maximize value from any worse hands like a lower set or two pair.

Top set with TT remains extremely strong on this Td6d8hAd board despite the Ad completing the diamond flush draw. Flash's line (BB 3-bet pre, flop donk bet then jam over my raise, turn small all-in bet of 1000 into 18450 pot) continues to be polarized toward nuts (Ad flush, unlikely sets like 88/66 given action), strong draws that hit, or bluffs/value thin. However, my equity is still favorable (>40% vs his range including missed draws, overpairs, weaker hands) due to history of loose aggressive play and frequent calls with marginal hands. Pot odds are exceptional (~18.5:1 implied), committing only 1000 (41% of my 2450 stack) to win a massive 19450+ pot. Folding a monster here is massively -EV. Raising all-in unnecessary as he's committed with 1000 shove and pot already huge; calling realizes equity perfectly, preserves 1450 for river control/value extraction if he had something behind (but stack shows 0). Long-term stack maximization favors continuing with this equity advantage 3-handed.

Three-handed, on the button with K♦3♣. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. In short-handed play I should be opening a much wider range from the button — K3o is marginal but fine to raise for fold equity and to avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot. Flash (SB) is deep and can defend wide, Elon (BB) is very short (≈13.5bb) and likely to shove or fold. I’ll open-raise to the table standard (300 total). This size is big enough to apply pressure and often take the blinds, while keeping the pot manageable if Elon shoves. If Elon jams, I’ll fold post-jam more often than call because K3o has poor equity vs a short-stack shove; if I get heads-up vs Flash I’ll use position to c-bet and pressure.

I have KQs in the small blind. This is a very strong starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. Minni has raised from the button, which could be a wide range. Elon, in the big blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 1350 chips (13.5 big blinds). If I raise, I am essentially committing to a pot against Elon if he jams, and I want to put pressure on Minni. Given Elon's stack size, I will 3-bet to 1000 to isolate or build the pot with my premium equity.

Short stack of 1350 (~13.5BB). Facing button open to 300 and SB 3-bet to 1000. K8o is too weak to call or shove against likely strong 3-bet range from Flash and Minni's calling range. Calling commits nearly all stack (900 to call, 450 left) with marginal king-high offsuit, poor postflop playability. Shoving 1450 total risks stack vs deep opponents who can call wide due to ICM pressure on me. Better to fold and wait for premium spot to ladder up.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♦3♣ to steal the blinds in 3-handed play, but Flash’s sizable 3-bet to 1000 makes this a marginal spot. Pot is 1400 and I must call 700, which requires ~33% equity to break even. K3o is heavily dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range (lots of A-x, Kx with better kickers and pocket pairs) and won’t have the needed equity often. A light 4‑bet is risky — I don’t have the right blockers and Flash is deep enough to call or shove. Post‑flop I’d face difficult decisions out of position in a bloated pot. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am the chip leader on the button in a three-handed game. With As 4d, I have a standard opening hand. Elon is very short-stacked in the small blind with only 1,300 chips (13 BBs). By raising to 300, I put pressure on him to either commit his stack or fold. If Elon shoves, I am likely priced into a call given the pot odds and the strength of Ace-high in a short-handed environment. Minni, in the big blind, has a significant stack but has shown a tendency to fold to aggression when not holding a premium hand. Raising here maintains my aggressive image and aims to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position.

Short stack with 13BB. Qc7d is a trash hand with queen-high, offsuit, no straight or flush potential. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands. Calling 250 into 450 pot offers poor implied odds given the hand's weakness and likely domination by raiser's range. 3-bet shoving 1300 is an overplay for this holding as it performs poorly against calling ranges. Folding preserves the stack for better opportunities against looser opens or in position.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with A♥8♠. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (I’ll have 300 committed). With deep stacks and heads-up this is a standard defend: A8o blocks a lot of Ax combinations and has reasonable equity vs a typical BTN opening range. I don’t want to 3-bet and polarize with a marginal hand — calling preserves fold equity on many flops and lets me realize equity postflop. Plan: call, see the flop, play aggressively with an Ace or good draws and fold to overwhelming aggression when I miss.

Heads-up vs a button opener on a very dry 7♥-3♠-6♦ flop, my A♥8♠ has good high-card value and blocks many A× combos in the opponent’s range. The raiser’s range (broadways, Ax, small pairs, suited connectors) misses a lot here, so I have decent fold equity. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to build a huge pot with just Ace-high — but I do want to take the initiative and fold out weaker holdings (Kx, Qx, small pairs, pure misses). A half‑pot continuation bet is the right balance: it extracts value when I’m ahead, applies pressure, and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: bet ½ pot (325). If he raises big, assume a very strong hand and fold; if he calls, proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw on a dry, low-card board (7-3-6 rainbow). Minni's lead of 325 into me (the pre-flop aggressor) is a 'donk bet' that often represents a marginal pair, a straight draw like 54, or a total bluff. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, and since I am in position with the chip lead, calling is the best play. I don't want to raise and risk a large portion of my stack against the only other big stack at the table with just Ace-high, but folding is too weak given my hand strength and position. Calling allows me to evaluate the turn and see if Minni continues her aggression.

Turn summary: board 7♥-3♠-6♦-2♥, I hold A♥8♠, pot 1,300 and it’s checked to me. Assessment: I have Ace-high (no pair) but I block Ax combos and the turn is a fairly clean, low card that doesn’t improve many of the hands that would have called my flop bet (most calling hands are single-pair combos like 7x/6x/3x, weak Ax, or floats). With three players remaining and deep stacks, I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to weaker parts of his range while keeping the sizing small enough to fold to a genuine raise. Plan: continuation-bet a size that folds out Kx/Qx and some small pairs but keeps the pot controllable if called. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I will re-evaluate on the river; if he raises big I’ll assume a made hand and fold. Action: bet ~½ pot (650).

On a turn board of 7h-3s-6d-2h, Minni has led out for a second time (a 'double donk' bet). This sequence on a low, connected board texture strongly suggests that she has connected with the small cards in her Big Blind defending range, likely holding a pair (7x, 6x), a straight (54), or two pair. My hand, As-4d, is currently just Ace-high. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), the mathematical probability of hitting it on the river is only about 8-9%. Minni's bet of 650 into a 1,300 pot gives me 3:1 odds, requiring roughly 25% equity to call profitably. Given that this board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's range over my Button opening range, and her continued aggression indicates a made hand, I do not have the required equity or sufficient implied odds to justify a call against the only other significant stack at the table. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my chip lead.

Short stack of 13BB on the button with AJo, a strong hand from this position. Standard play is to shove all-in preflop to leverage fold equity against deep-stacked blinds and deny them equity if called. AJ has good equity against typical calling ranges (e.g., ~40-50% vs loose Ax, pairs, broadways). Min-raising or calling risks difficult postflop spots OOP with short stack. Opponents have been aggressive but deep stacks make them less incentivized to call off light. Maximizes EV over tournament.

Elon has shoved/all‑in for 1,300 and I must call 1,250 more to match. The pot is 1,450, so calling 1,250 would give me 2,700 to win — I need roughly 46% equity to justify a call. My hand 7♥2♣ is the worst starting hand with very poor equity vs any reasonable shove range (well below the ~46% break‑even). With a healthy stack (18,425) I should preserve chips for better spots rather than risk 1,250 in a weak coin‑flip/dominated situation. Fold and wait for a much stronger spot.

Elon has moved all-in for 1300 chips from the button. After Minni folds, I am in the big blind with T4o and need to call 1200 more. The pot is 1450, meaning I am getting approximately 1.2:1 odds on a call. To break even, I need roughly 45.3% equity. Against a standard 13BB shoving range from the button (which usually includes any pair, any Ace, most Kings, and many suited connectors/gappers), T4o typically has around 34-36% equity. Since my hand is significantly behind the required equity threshold and T4o is a very weak starting hand, the correct play is to fold and preserve my chip lead.

I’m on the button with 9♣7♠. SB (Flash) is very deep, BB (Elon) is short (~13.5bb). In position with a marginal but playable hand, I want to apply pressure to the short stack and try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable facing a deep SB. A standard 3x open (to 300) gives good fold equity and isolates Elon — if he shoves I can call comfortably: his shove would leave me needing to call ~1,050 more to win a pot of ~2,750, so break-even equity is ~38%; against a short-stack shove range 9-7o is around or above that threshold and the call is a small fraction of my 18425 stack. If Flash 3-bets big out of position I can fold and wait for a better spot. Therefore I open-raise to 300 to take advantage of position and pressure the short blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 7d, a marginal hand. Minni has raised from the button. Elon, the Big Blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 13.5 big blinds (1350 chips) remaining. If I call here, I am inviting a potential shove from Elon, and I would be playing out of position against Minni if the hand continues. 97o is too weak to defend in this specific configuration, especially with a short stack behind me likely to jam. Folding is the safest and most efficient play to preserve my chip lead.

9s3c is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and no flush potential. Facing a button raise from Minni (loose-aggressive based on history), out of position in BB. Pot odds: calling 200 into 450 (~2.25:1), but equity against her wide button range is too low (<30%) to justify. Stack of 1350 is short, but preserve for better spots. Standard fold.

I am currently the chip leader with over 20,000 chips. The player in the Small Blind (Elon) is extremely short-stacked with only 1,300 chips (13 big blinds). With Trey-Six of diamonds, although it is suited, it is a very weak hand. Raising here often induces a shove from a short stack like Elon, and I do not want to be forced to fold after putting chips in or call a shove with such a weak holding. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my lead and wait for a better spot to eliminate the short stack.

Short-stacked with 13BB in SB after BTN fold. T8o is within standard shoving range vs BB (includes many offsuit broadways and connectors like T8o for fold equity and playability). BB defends wide but has to call 12.5BB effectively into growing pot; high fold equity + decent equity if called maximizes EV over tournament survival.

Elon has raised to 1,300 from the SB (leaving only 50 behind), so this is effectively a short-stack shove/iso from him. The pot is 1,400 and I must put in 1,200 more to call, which requires roughly 46% equity to break even. Ks7h is frequently dominated by the shoving range (Axs, better Kx, and pocket pairs) and won’t have the equity needed vs a reasonable short-stack push. A tiny min‑raise is meaningless and just bloats the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and fold, waiting for a better spot.

Short stack with 14.5BB on the button facing unopened pot. J7o is a playable open-raise hand from this position due to fold equity against the blinds, who have shown aggression but also frequent folds pre-flop in history. Raising builds the pot or steals blinds frequently. Position post-flop if called. Equity decent vs calling ranges (blocks high card combos). Will c-bet most flops or fold to 3-bet. Avoid limping or calling small as it commits too much of short stack awkwardly. Fold too tight preserves stack but misses EV+ steal spots.

K2o (Kd2s) is a very weak, easily dominated hand facing a button open. I’m out of position postflop (SB vs Button) and the big blind is deep-stacked and likely to call or 3-bet, which would create a multiway pot where K2 has very little equity. Calling 250 to play OOP with a poor kicker isn’t worth it, and a small 3-bet won’t isolate reliably and only bloats the pot. Preserve my 18k+ stack for better, cleaner spots — fold preflop.

Elon is extremely short-stacked (1,150 chips remaining) and his raise to 300 represents a significant portion of his stack. While J4 suited has some equity, it is generally a weak hand to defend with against a range that is likely looking to commit. Given my large stack, I can afford to fold and wait for a more dominant hand to eliminate the short stack rather than calling with a hand that is likely dominated or forced to play for the remainder of his chips on most flops.

I’m on the button with 4♣5♣ (suited connector) and deep stacks (my 18,425 vs SB Flash 19,925 and very short BB Elon 1,500). The pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. 4-5s is a good speculative, position-play hand, but raising here risks inducing an all‑in from Elon (1500) or a big isolation from Flash and creates needless variance. Calling 100 keeps me in position to see a flop, realizes my hand’s equity, and lets me play postflop with initiative. If I face a shove or large 3‑bet preflop I’ll fold (or call only if pot odds/reads justify). On favourable flops I’ll continue aggressively; otherwise I’ll check/fold to heavy action.

I hold AKo, which is a premier starting hand. Minni has limped from the button, and Elon is sitting in the big blind with a very short stack of 1500 chips. I want to raise for value and to potentially isolate. If Elon chooses to shove his remaining stack, I am comfortably calling. Raising to 450 builds a significant pot and punishes the button limp.

3s4h is a very weak offsuit hand with minimal playability. Facing a button limp and small blind raise to 450 (350 to call into 650 pot), the pot odds are insufficient given my out-of-position status postflop. Flash's SB raise range is wide but still crushes this hand, and Minni can wake up with a strong hand. Equity is low against any continuation, and with only 15BB stack (1500 at 50/100), better to preserve chips for premium spots in late tournament stages rather than speculative calls with trash. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop situations.

Call to 450 (add 350). With 4♣5♣ on the button and deep stacks, calling the SB’s raise is the lowest-variance, highest-EV line — I keep position, realize equity, and buy myself the implied odds to hit straights/flushes. Pot math: I’m risking 350 to win ~1000 (post-call), so I need ≈35% equity; against a typical SB open-after-limp range my hand has playable equity and excellent postflop playability. A 3-bet/raise here would create unnecessary variance and can induce a big isolation or 3‑bet; folding throws away fold equity and table position. Postflop plan: play aggressively on low/wet boards and with real draws or pairs (semi-bluffs, leads/raises when appropriate), control the pot on high-ace/king boards, and fold to heavy aggression when missed. If Flash shows extreme strength (large 3‑bet or shove) I’ll fold unless pot odds/reads justify calling.

I have AKo on a paired board of 7h-3h-7d. This is a favorable board to continuation bet as the pre-flop aggressor because it is difficult for my opponent, Minni, to have connected with this board unless she holds a 7 or a pocket pair. Betting here maintains the initiative and can fold out many of her air hands or suited connectors that missed. I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw with the King of Hearts. A bet of 400 (40% of the pot) is a standard size to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called.

Board: 7♥ 3♥ 7♦, my hand 4♣5♣. Flash bets 400 into a 1400 pot (400 to call). Pot odds = 400 / (1400 + 400) ≈ 22.2% needed to break even. My only realistic outs are the four 6s to make 3-4-5-6-7 (≈8.5% to hit on the turn, ≈16% to hit by river). I have no backdoor flush (with two clubs in hand and zero clubs on the flop it’s impossible to make a club flush by the river), so my true equity is well below the required ~22%. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — the paired sevens + two hearts board strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range and I don’t block important hands, so my raise would be hard to credibly represent and would create unnecessary variance. With deep stacks there are some implied odds if I hit, but not enough to overcome the immediate pot‑odds deficit and the likelihood of running into continuing aggression. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold missed flops to pressure, the correct, low-variance play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8h 4s, a very weak hand. While I am the chip leader, Elon is currently short-stacked in the small blind with only about 14.5 big blinds. A short stack in the blinds is very likely to shove over a button opening raise to try and double up. Raising with such a weak hand into a potential shove is unprofitable, and limping is generally poor strategy. Given my stack advantage and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to play a sub-optimal hand here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Ad3s is a weak ace with a terrible kicker and no suited or connected potential. In the small blind facing the big blind after button fold, completing has poor implied odds and post-flop playability is disastrous against a wide BB defending range. With 14.5BB stack, preserve for better spots rather than risk it on marginal equity. Folding maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Short-stacked at 14.5BB on the button facing blinds with massive stacks (180BB+). J2 suited is a weak hand with poor playability and equity against likely calling ranges from blinds, who defend wide vs short stacks. Standard push-fold charts from button exclude J2s; better to wait for a stronger spot to preserve stack and ladder up in tournament. Folding is disciplined and maximizes EV long-term over 150 hands.

Hand is 9♦2♦ in the small blind against only the big blind (Flash). This is a very weak hand, but it’s cheap to see the flop — I only need to add 50 more chips. Raising as a steal is unlikely to work versus a deep, aggressive BB and would bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll complete the blind (call) to 100 total and proceed cautiously post‑flop: fold to heavy action unless I pair up or pick up a strong draw.

I am in the Big Blind with QhJs. Elon has folded on the button, and Minni has completed the small blind. QJ offsuit is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario and is likely ahead of Minni's limping range. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define the hand further while in position for the rest of the hand.

Facing a raise to 300 from the big blind with 9♦2♦ in the small blind. I already posted 50 so I need 200 more (300 total) to see the flop; the pot is 400 so calling 200 to make the pot 600 gives ~33% break‑even equity. 9‑2 suited is a very weak hand but the price is small and, with only the BB left, I'll be in position post‑flop — so I’ll take a cheap flop with the ability to fold to pressure. I won't 3‑bet (hand is too marginal) and I’ll fold to significant post‑flop aggression unless I pair or pick up a real diamond/straight draw. Call 300 to see the flop and proceed cautiously.

Flop: 5♦ 7♣ 8♥ — my hand 9♦2♦. I have no pair, only a weak backdoor diamond flush (need both turn+river diamonds — ~4% to complete) and a backdoor straight possibility (a 6 completes 5-6-7-8-9 — ~16% by river). I also have ~24% to pair one of my hole cards by the river. Against Flash (the preflop raiser) this is a marginal holding and I should avoid bloating the pot out of position. Deep stacks mean preserving chips and realizing equity is preferable to bluff-leading. Plan: check and keep the pot small; call a small-to-medium c-bet because pot odds will often justify it, but fold to large pressure unless I improve or pick up a real draw (pair, a diamond plus additional equity, or the straight).

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am making a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) into this coordinated board. While the 5-7-8 texture is favorable for a small blind's limping range, a bet here maintains my initiative and forces Minni to fold her air and weak hands that missed the flop. If I am called, I still have two overcards (Q, J) that provide six outs to top pair, and if she raises, I can easily fold without further investment.

I’m OOP with 9♦2♦ on 5♦ 7♣ 8♥ — no pair, only backdoor diamond flush (needs both turn+river) and a backdoor straight with a 6 (turn or river). Flash bet 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 gives ~25% break‑even pot odds. My chance to improve to a competitive hand by the river (pair / straight / backdoor flush) is in the mid‑20s/low‑30s, and Flash is a preflop raiser who c‑bets a wide range, so there are many worse hands I can still beat. I’ll take the cheap look: call to realize equity but avoid bloating the pot — I won’t raise with this marginal holding out of position. Plan after the call: fold to large turn pressure unless I pair or pick up a real draw (a 6 to make a straight, a diamond that meaningfully increases flush chances, or a pair), and play aggressively only if I improve.

Out of position with 9♦2♦ on 5♦ 7♣ 8♥ 5♥. I haven’t improved — the backdoor diamond flush is dead now (turn wasn’t a diamond) and I only have 4 outs (three 6s) to make the straight plus 6 outs to pair a hole card (three 9s + three 2s) = 10 outs ≈ 21–22% to improve on the river. The pot is already 1,200 and there is currently no bet in front of me, so betting here would bloat the pot with a very marginal hand and get called by plenty of better hands in Flash’s wide c-betting range. I’ll keep the pot small and see the river: check and re-evaluate. If Flash bets the river small enough that my ~22% equity gets correct pot odds I can call, but I’ll fold to large river aggression. No raise or bluff from me on this street.

The turn card is a five, pairing the bottom card of the flop. I currently hold Queen-high with no direct draw. Minni called my continuation bet on a flop of 5-7-8, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board, such as a pair of sevens or eights, or a straight draw. By checking back here, I take a free card to try and hit a Queen or Jack on the river, which would likely give me the best hand. Betting again as a bluff is risky because the five is a card that could easily have improved a small blind's defending range, and she is unlikely to fold a made pair given the action so far.

Board is 5♦ 7♣ 8♥ 5♥ 8♦; I hold 9♦2♦ — no pair, no flush (only four diamonds between my hand and board), so my showdown value is very weak vs most of Flash’s likely range. Flash opened pre and c‑bet the flop then checked turn (and checked river when action folded to me) — classic line for a wide c‑bet range that missed. Pot = 1,200. A small-sized bluff pressures many of those missed overcards and medium hands (A‑x, K‑x, Q‑x) that will check back and fold to a bet while risking little if called. Betting 400 (~33% pot) keeps risk low; with reasonable showdown equity (~20–25%) the break‑even fold frequency is only ~18–25%, so a small bluff here is +EV. If called I’m usually beaten and will cut losses; if it folds I pick up the 1,200 pot. I’ll make the small bluff to try to take it down.

The board has run out with two pair (Eights and Fives) and a Seven. My hand is Queen-high, which loses to any pair and even many Ace-high hands in Minni's range. Minni checked the flop and turn, then led for 400 on the river after the board double-paired. This bet is small (1/4 pot), which could either be a 'thin' value bet from a Seven or an Ace-high hand trying to get to showdown cheaply, or a blocker bet. However, my Queen-high has almost zero showdown value. I would need to raise as a bluff to win, but given that the board has double-paired, Minni might feel committed with any piece of the board. Calling is not an option as I cannot win the pot with Queen-high at showdown. Fold is the most disciplined play.

I’m on the button with K3s — a playable, suited king with good positional advantage. With only two players left (big blind is a short 13.5BB stack, small blind is deep and aggressive), I want to seize the initiative and steal the blinds while preserving fold equity. A standard 3x open to 300 achieves that: it’s large enough to discourage some callers from the SB but not so large that I’m overcommitting if the short BB shoves. If Elon (BB) jams, his range will be wide and K3s has reasonable equity vs a shove; if Flash (SB) 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate or fold since K3s is marginal out of position. Raise to 300 to take the lead and exploit position.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised from the button, and Elon in the Big Blind is very short-stacked with only 1,350 chips. If I call, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, and I risk a shove from Elon. Given the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to commit any chips to this pot.

Short stack with 13.5BB, in BB facing aggressive button raise from Minni to 3BB. J2s is a marginal suited hand but too weak OOP vs button range. Calling leaves awkward SPR ~1.8 postflop. Shoving risks being called by better (e.g., any Jx, 22+, Ax) where equity <40%. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 32 hands left; Minni's history shows frequent button steals but we wait for premium.

9-7 offsuit is a marginal hand. With Elon being extremely short-stacked in the Small Blind (13 BB), he is in a 'shove or fold' range. Raising here would likely induce a shove from him, which I cannot profitably call with this hand. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my chip lead and avoid unnecessary variance against the short stack.

Short-stacked at 13BB in SB with 3d6d (suited connector). Flash folds button, heads-up vs BB Minni who plays aggressively but has posted BB. Standard short-stack strategy from SB vs BB: wide shoving range includes 63s due to fold equity, decent equity vs calling range (~35-40% vs loose defenders), and potential to flop strong hands like straights/flushes/sets. Calling 50 leaves awkward postflop stack; folding too tight and misses dead money (pot 150). Shoving maximizes fold equity while building stack through blinds/chips accumulation in tournament with goal to max stack over 150 hands. Minni calls stations but shoves have worked historically.

Elon opened to 1300 from the small blind and I only have to call 1200 more (1300 total). I hold K♦7♥ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. My stack (18,675) is deep relative to the cost (1,200 ≈ 6.4% of my stack), and Elon’s remaining effective stack is essentially negligible (shows 50), so there’s little value in making a bigger raise or a bluff-3-bet here. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop against what is likely a wide/short-stacked raising range, and preserves my ability to fold if I completely miss or extract value if I pair my king. I’ll call 1300 total to see the flop.

Stack critically short at 50 chips (0.5BB) into 2600 pot post-flop (~52:1 to call if checked/raised). Flopped top pair (6s) with 3d6d on 6h-2s-4h board, providing showdown value and ~35-45% equity vs Minni's calling range (overpairs, sets, two-pair, draws like hearts/wheel). Drawy board favors aggression for protection/fold equity. History shows Minni defends wide but folds to post-flop pressure often. Shoving extracts value from worse (77-JJ, Ax suited, flush/straight draws) while denying equity to better hands. Check risks free cards or check-behind losing pot; no fold possible with tiny stack. Optimal short-stack play: jam for max EV, aligning with tournament goal of stack accumulation.

Elon has bet only 50 into a 2,650 pot and appears effectively pot‑committed/very short‑stacked. I hold K♦7♥ on 6♥‑2♠‑4♥: no pair but I have 6 clear outs to pair (3 kings + 3 sevens) — roughly ~24% to hit by the river — plus a small extra from backdoor hearts. Calling 50 is a trivial, +EV decision given the pot odds (50 into ~2,700 ≈ 1.9% break‑even) and there’s no value in raising since Elon can’t fold more chips and there are no other players in. I’ll call the 50 and see the turn.

Preflop on the button with J8o, a speculative hand with decent high-card value and suited potential (though offsuit here). Position is excellent, blinds posted (pot 150), no prior action indicated so folded to me. Standard button steal range includes this. Raise to 300 (3x BB) for value/fold equity against likely folding blinds based on history where they defend selectively. Postflop playability if called, deep stacks allow maneuvering. Folding or limping suboptimal; limping invites multiway pots.

I'm in the small blind with 7♣2♠ — the worst non-foldable hand. Elon opened to 300 (pot 450) and I must call 250. Calling 250 into a 450 pot (700 total after my call) requires ~35.7% equity to break even, which 7-2 offsuit does not have versus a button raising range (and it plays terribly out of position). A light 3-bet/bluff here is also unattractive: the big blind is yet to act and I have no postflop playability or blockers to make it profitable. I have a healthy stack and many hands left — folding preserves chips for real spots. EV-optimal play is to fold.

I have Pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand. Elon is the short stack with only 24 big blinds and has opened from the button. Given his stack size and the strength of my hand, I want to 3-bet to build the pot and potentially get him to commit his entire stack. A raise to 900 is a standard 3-bet size that is likely to be called by his continuing range or induce a shove from his short stack.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 (adding 800) from Flash in the BB after my button raise to 300 with J8o. Pot is 1250, to call 600 (pot odds ~2.08:1, requiring ~33% equity). J8o is a standard BTN open but marginal vs BB 3-bet range, which is polarized strong (JJ+, AKo/s, some suited broadways/connectors) or bluffs. Offsuit removes flush potential, and while I have position heads-up, postflop SPR would be low (~2400 effective into ~1850 pot post-call = ~1.3 SPR), committing me with a mediocre hand unlikely to realize equity well against Flash's range. Flash has shown aggression (frequent raises/C-bets) but BB 3-bets indicate strength; history shows they defend selectively and value bet rivers. With 24BB stack late in tournament (hand 120/150), preserve chips for better spots like premiums or steals vs weaker ranges rather than speculative call/3-bet (min-raise to 1500 uses half stack with poor equity). Folding maximizes EV long-term.

I’m on the button with Qc6c in a three‑handed pot. With blinds 50/100 the pot is 150 and I have position on both opponents. Elon (BB) is short (~2,300, ~23bb) and Flash (SB) is very deep — this is a spot to be aggressive and try to steal or isolate the short stack. A standard open to 300 (3x the BB here, table standard) gives good fold equity and lets me play postflop in position if someone calls. Plan: raise to 300 to pressure Elon and force him to commit or play a pot heads‑up with me; if Flash defends I’ll proceed cautiously on unfavorable flops but use position to control the pot. If Elon jams from the BB I’m willing to call (he will often have a wide shove range and I have reasonable showdown equity), but I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet from Flash that commits me out of position with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-5 offsuit. Minni, who is active on the button, has raised to 300. With a very short stack in the Big Blind (Elon with 2300), calling here would be poor strategy as I would be playing a weak hand out of position, and I risk being squeezed by a shove from Elon. The hand is simply not strong enough to continue against a standard opening raise.

Pocket kings is the best hand preflop except AA. With a short stack of 2300 (23BB), facing a button minraise from Minni who has a large stack and raises frequently based on history, shoving all-in maximizes fold equity, gets value from worse hands, and denies equity to draws if called. Calling is too passive and leaves me vulnerable postflop. Folding is never correct here.

Elon (BB) has shoved to 2,300 after my standard 300 open. He’s a short stack (~23bb) so his shove range is wide — many pairs, Ax, Kx and some suited connectors — and I’m getting ~43% pot odds (call 2,000 into a ~2,650 pot), so I need ~43% equity to break even. Qc6c is marginal but suited, has a Queen blocker and decent equity vs a wide shove, and I keep positional advantage post‑flop. Raising above his all‑in is pointless (creates a side pot I don’t need since Flash folded) and folding here is too nitty given the pot odds and Elon's range. Plan: call to isolate the short stack, play in position — fold to heavy action if I don’t improve, or continue aggressively when I pick up equity. Therefore I will call, putting a total of 2,300 into the pot.

KK remains an overpair to a dry flop (2d Jc 6h) with no flush draws completed and limited straight draws. With only 100 chips left (2% pot on a 4650 pot), this is a mandatory shove for thin value against Minni's wide calling range from the button after flat-calling my preflop 3-bet shove. She likely has overcards, pocket pairs below kings, or broadway cards that we dominate. Checking risks giving her a free card or missing value; shoving maximizes EV as the pot odds for her are irrelevant and fold equity is minimal but value is huge from worse hands. History shows Minni calls large sizes with marginal holdings, increasing thin value likelihood.

Flop is 2♦ J♣ 6♥ and I hold Q♣6♣ — I’ve paired my 6 with a decent Q kicker. Elon shoved a very wide range preflop, so I’m ahead of many hands (overcards, weaker pairs, many Ax/Kx combos) and only losing to Jx, bigger pairs and some rare hands. Facing a tiny 100 bet into ~4,750 gives excellent pot odds and I have position, so folding would be too nitty and raising is unnecessary against his likely range. I’ll call the 100 to see the turn and control the pot; that makes my total committed 2,400.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from this position. I'll raise to 300 to pressure the blinds and take initiative. My stack size allows me to play aggressively against the remaining two players, and T9o has decent playability if I am called.

3-handed, facing a Button raise to 3x from Flash, who has been aggressive in recent hands (e.g., frequent raises and postflop aggression like in #108, #120). My 3s5d is offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor playability and low equity (~30-35% vs a wide Button range). SB defend range vs BTN open is wide but 35o is trash - minimal straight potential, no flush draw suited, dominated often. Calling 250 into 450 pot (5.5:1 but poor implied odds postflop with my short-ish stack of 48BB and opponents' deep stacks). Raising has no fold equity with this hand. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in late tournament stage (hand 122/150), where survival and better hands matter more.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and Elon folded. I’m on the big blind with Tc7d. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (300 total committed). Against a button open this hand has reasonable equity and the pot odds (~3.25:1) justify defending. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a marginal T7o (gives up position and invites larger decisions), so I’ll just call and see a flop. Post-flop I’ll proceed cautiously: fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, but play for value/raise when I pair a Ten or Seven or pick up strong draws.

Check. On Ah-8c-8s with Tc7d I have no pair and only six live outs (three Tens, three Sevens). Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop — that range includes many Ax hands, pocket pairs (including 88) and some broadways. Betting OOP has almost no value (won’t fold many A‑x or pairs) and bloats the pot with a very weak hand. Best line is pot control and fold preservation: check and give up to a reasonable bet. I’ll only continue vs a very small, obvious blocker/steal bet; otherwise I’ll fold to significant aggression and re-evaluate if checked to on the turn.

On a board of Ace-Eight-Eight, as the pre-flop aggressor, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. The Ace favors my perceived range significantly. While I have no direct pair or draw, Minni's check is indicative of a weak range or a marginal hand that might fold to pressure. A small bet of roughly one-third of the pot (225) should be enough to take this down now or set up further barrels if called.

Call 225 (525 total committed). The flop bet is small relative to the pot, and my six outs (three Tens, three Sevens) give ~24% equity to improve to the river. I only need ~20.5% equity (225 to win ~1100) so pot odds + equity make a call +EV here. Flash’s button c-bet range is wide (lots of A-x, some missed broadways and medium pairs); a small c-bet often contains many hands I can still beat when I hit. I don’t want to 3-bet/raise OOP with T7o — that bloats the pot with a marginal hand and forfeits positional advantage. Plan: call and see the turn; fold to significant turn aggression if I miss, otherwise re-evaluate (check-control if checked to, start value-betting if I pair a Ten/Seven or pick up strong redraws).

Board: Ah 8c 8s Qh. I hold Tc7d — no pair. I have 6 clean outs (three Tens, three Sevens) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Pot is 1,100 and I’m not facing a bet right now.

Calling a turn bet is only +EV if the bet is very small. Break‑even call fraction is B/(P+2B); with P=1,100 that means I can only profitably call roughly B ≤ ~194 chips. Flash is the preflop raiser and c‑bet small on the flop, so his continuing range contains A‑x, 88, Qx and some missed broadways — he’s unlikely to bet tiny enough often enough for me to call profitably with only 13% equity. A raise/bluff OOP here is a poor play: I have minimal fold equity and would be committing chips with a weak draw against a stacked opponent.

Plan: check and keep the pot under control. If Flash checks back I see a free river and can realize equity. If he fires a small probe (≈≤200) I’ll call for the pot odds; if he bets anything sizable (>~200) I’ll fold. If I pair a Ten or Seven on the river I’ll seek thin value; if I miss and he barrels, I’ll fold.

On a board of Ace-Eight-Eight-Queen, the turn card (Queen) is a scare card that likely improved some of Minni's flop-calling range (such as QJ, QT, or KQ) or didn't change the fact that she likely has an Ace or an Eight given her call on the flop. Since I have Ten-high with no draw and no showdown value, continuing to bluff into a range that already called an Ace-high paired board is high risk. Checking back allows me to see a free river and potentially evaluate her action on the final street, preserving my stack as the chip leader.

I missed my six outs (T/7) on the river and have no pair or showdown value with Tc7d on Ah-8c-8s-Qh-4s. Flash (button) was the preflop raiser, c-bet a small half‑pot on the flop, then checked turn and checked the river — a line consistent with a wide c-bet range that still contains lots of A‑x and medium pairs that will call a river probe. To make a profitable bluff I need fold equity f > B/(P+B) (e.g. a 300 bet needs >21% folds, 600 needs >35%). Given his passive check‑back line, I don’t trust I’ll get that fold frequency often enough, and I block very few of his calling combinations. With decent stack depth and no value, preserving chips is the correct play. I’ll check and show down (or fold to a river bet earlier if faced with one).

On the river, the board is Ah-8c-8s-Qh-4s. I have Ten-high (9c-Ts) and absolutely no showdown value. However, Minni called my continuation bet on the flop (A88), which strongly suggests she has at least a piece of the board—most likely an Ace, an Eight, or a pocket pair. While she checked the turn and the river, my check back on the turn likely signaled that I don't have a powerhouse. If I bet now, I would be trying to represent a slow-played Ace or a Queen that checked for pot control, but it is unlikely to fold out an Ace or even a Queen in this spot. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, there is no need to risk additional chips on a low-frequency bluff when the opponent's range is likely to call. I will check and concede the pot.
