I’ll call 100 to keep the pot manageable and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to significant turn aggression, but continue if I pair (3 or 6) or pick up a clear bluffing opportunity. Raising would be overplaying a very weak hand; folding here concedes the pot too cheaply. Calling preserves low variance and keeps options open.

Board K♠-4♣-8♥-Q♦ with my 3♦6♠: I have no pair and no realistic draw. I’m out of position vs Flash (bigger stack) who c-bet the flop; betting myself here would be a pure bluff with very few meaningful blockers and would bloat a pot I’m likely to fold to pressure in. Checking controls the pot and keeps better hands and thin bluffs in. If Flash bets the turn I’ll fold to significant aggression and only consider calling very cheap turn bets (roughly ≤100 into the 400 pot) to see a free-ish river; if he checks back I’ll evaluate the river for a realistic bluff or any pair/improvement. Preserve chips and avoid high-variance bluffs with no equity is the +EV line.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Kings with a Trey). The turn Queen is a potential scare card that could have improved Minni's range (hands like QJ, QT, or even a slow-played King). Since I am the significant chip leader and the tournament is nearing its end, I will prioritize pot control. Checking back avoids the risk of a check-raise and allows me to reach showdown or evaluate a river bet more safely.

I have 3♦6♠ with no pair and no realistic draw on K♠-4♣-8♥-Q♦-4♦. Flash c-bet a small 100 on the flop then checked the turn, a line consistent with a wide/marginal or medium-strength hand; the river pairing the 4 only makes many opponent hands (4x, Qx, Kx, 8x, etc.) better. I have essentially zero showdown value and very few meaningful blockers, so a river bet would be a pure bluff with low fold equity versus a deeper, reasonably passive opponent. The low-variance +EV play is to check and preserve chips — I’ll fold to any reasonable bet and only continue if Flash checks back (see showdown).

I have top pair which became two pair (Kings and Fours) on the river. My opponent limped from the small blind and has checked on every street, including after the river paired the bottom card. While I could potentially extract thin value from a Queen or an 8, checking back is the safest and most professional play given my massive chip lead and the fact that there are only six hands remaining in the tournament. Checking guarantees a showdown, avoids the risk of a check-raise from a slow-played hand or a better King, and ensures I maintain my dominant position as the tournament concludes.

I have A♥K♦ on the button — a premium hand with position and deep stacks (≈101 BB). Raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to take the initiative, build the pot when ahead, and reduce the chance of both blinds seeing cheap flops. A min-raise to 200 is too small and invites loose calls; a standard 3x isolates and keeps SPR reasonable for postflop play. If I face a moderate 3-bet I’ll consider calling to leverage position; against a very large 3-bet/shove I’ll evaluate stack-to-pot odds and villain tendencies, but my default is to raise here for value and fold equity.

I am holding 7s 5h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing a 3x open raise from the Button (Minni). Being out of position for the rest of the hand against the Button makes this an easy fold, particularly since I have a significant chip lead and do not need to defend wide in this spot.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). Hand is 5s4d, offsuit low connector with minimal playability OOP heads-up. Pot 450, call 200 for ~3.25:1 odds, but equity against Minni's likely wide button range (~35-40% hands) is around 30-35%, not great, and postflop OOP dynamics are poor with this hand (few strong draws or made hands). Late in tournament (hand 145/150), stacks deep (96bb effective), prioritize preserving stack over speculative calls with trash. Minni raises frequently from history, but folding avoids difficult spots. Standard fold.

With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial chip lead (nearly double the stacks of my opponents), my primary objective is to avoid unnecessary risks. J4o is a very weak hand to play from the button. While I could attempt a steal, the potential gain does not outweigh the risk of being caught in a pot with a marginal hand against players who may feel pressured to accumulate chips. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my lead.

Hand 146/150, short-handed 3 players left, deep stacks (~95BB). In SB with T9o vs BB (Minni), button (Flash) folded. T9o is a strong SB stealing hand: good playability, straight potential, decent equity vs calling range. Standard to raise wide from SB vs BB for fold equity and to build pot OOP. Minni defends wide based on history but often folds to aggression preflop. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is balanced size for position and dynamics. Limping too passive late tournament; folding too tight.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the small blind with 6♠3♣ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (total commit = 300). Calling gives me pot odds of 200 into a 600 pot (~33% equity). 6‑3o is a weak, often‑dominated hand and bad to 3‑bet OOP — I don’t want to bloat the pot or play a big postflop pot out of position with junk. However this is heads‑up and opens are wide, so defending by calling to see a flop is standard: I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and fold to significant aggression on unfavorable flops or proceed if I pair or pick up a draw. Total amount to commit (including my posted BB) = 300.

Flop Js Jd 3s is a paired board with a flush draw possible (two spades). Preflop raiser OOP with T9o, which misses completely: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot absent), no flush draw. Equity is minimal (~10-20% vs BB calling range heavy on Jx, Ax suited spades, medium pairs). BB (Minni) defends wide but board favors her range. Deep stacks late tournament (146/150), but no reason to barrel air OOP here - poor bluff spot on paired, dryish board. Check to control pot, fold to most bets, seek free card or turn bluff if checked behind. Avoid bloating pot OOP with trash.

Check. I have a small pair (3s with a 6 kicker) on a J♠ J♦ 3♠ flop — a vulnerable bottom pair with a backdoor spade route but easily dominated by any J or many pocket pairs (QQ+, TT) that Elon could have after a SB open. Elon checked, so his range contains lots of missed overcards, medium pairs, slowplays and some draws; with my hand OOP I want to keep the pot small and avoid bloating it with a marginal holding. Checking lets me control the pot size and see how he behaves: I’ll call a small-to-medium continuation bet (to realize equity and fold only to large, polarized aggression), but I will fold to strong pressure since I’m often behind. If the turn pairs the board or gives me trips or a reasonable redraw, I’ll change gears and extract value; otherwise I’ll continue playing cautiously.

Turn board Js Jd 3s Qc pairs the board with possible straight draws and live spade flush draw. Initially appeared to miss but T9 actually has an open-ended straight draw (8 or K for QJT9 straight or broadway). ~32% equity if called (OESD). OOP first to act after Minni checked flop (weak range: floats, weak pairs, Ax/spades). Semi-bluff betting optimal: high fold equity vs her checking range, builds pot with equity, denies equity to her draws. Late tourney 3-handed (146/150), deep stacks (~93BB), aggression key to maximize stack. Size 2/3 pot (400) balances value (obv Jx/overpairs) and bluffs. Check risks giving free card to spades/draws or multipot showdown.

I have two pair (Jacks and Threes) on J♠ J♦ 3♠ Q♣ with 6♠3♣. That’s a strong but vulnerable holding — Qx (which just paired on the turn) and any J in hand (trips) beat me, and rare full houses/quads are possible. Elon checked the flop then made a relatively small turn bet (400 into a 1000 pot), which can include both thin value (Qx/KQ, some Jx) and a lot of medium-strength hands or probes. I’m in position, so I can keep control of the pot and see a river cheaply. Raising here would build the pot and commit chips against a range that contains many hands that currently beat me. I’ll call the 400 to keep the pot manageable, charge bluffs and worse hands, and re-evaluate on the river — folding to large aggression if Elon shows strong action on the next street. Total committed after calling = 300 (pre) + 400 = 700.

River 8s hits my open-ended straight draw from the turn (T9 now makes Q-J-T-9-8 straight). Strong value hand on this board, beating Jx (top pair), two-pair combinations without full house, and missed draws. Vulnerable to spade flushes (three spades on board) and full houses (e.g., JJx), but Minni's line (call pre, check flop paired board, call turn) is consistent with Jx, medium pairs, or weaker Qx rather than premium strength (which might bet flop). She checks behind weaker hands often per history. Deep stacks (89BB), 3-handed late (146/150), pot 1400: value bet to extract from her calling range (Jx, 88, QQ?). Sizing 900 (~2/3 pot) balances value and protection, induces calls from one-pair hands wary of flush but not folding top pair, while not pricing in monsters. Checking risks free showdown or her value betting; betting maximizes EV OOP.

I have 6♠3♣ on J♠ J♦ 3♠ Q♣ 8♠ — two pair (Jacks and Threes). The pot is 2300 and Elon’s river raise is 900, so calling 900 would commit 1600 more total (900 now) to win a final pot of 3200 — I need roughly 28% equity to break even. Elon’s line (SB open pre, check on a paired J‑3 flop, small turn bet on the Q, then a river raise) is consistent with a value‑heavy range: Qx that just paired the turn, any J (trips), rivered 8x, 3x/sets/full houses, and some spade flushes — many of which beat my two pair. The fraction of pure bluffs that I cleanly beat is likely small versus this line, and my hand has few blockers to remove his value combos. Raising myself would be committing a large chunk of my stack with a very vulnerable hand. With pot odds and the opponent’s polarized/value line against me, a fold is the +EV play — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

3-handed on the button with excellent position. Jh4s is a marginal hand but playable from BTN due to postflop potential with J-high and live cards. Blinds are deep, pot is small (150), no raises yet (limped pot). Steal attempt is high EV; opponents show passive tendencies in history with frequent checks postflop. Raise standard size to ~2.5x BB to build pot or take it down. Minni aggressive from SB but position allows control. Late in tournament (147/150), need to accumulate chips aggressively without spew.

Facing Elon’s button open to 250 from my small blind with J♦6♥. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to continue. J6o is a very weak, frequently dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability out of position. I’d need ~33% equity just to break even on a call heads‑up, and that required equity falls further when the big blind (Flash) can still enter or 3‑bet. A min‑raise to 400 as a bluff is thin here — I’ll still be OOP versus both players and likely get called or 3‑bet by deeper stacks. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am currently the chip leader with a nearly 10,000-chip advantage and only four hands remaining in the tournament. While 6-5 offsuit technically has the pot odds to justify a defense of the big blind against a button raise (needing ~27% equity), the strategic priority at this stage is to minimize variance and protect my lead. Playing a speculative hand out of position against Elon, who has been aggressive, creates unnecessary risk of losing a significant pot. I will fold and maintain my stack.

I'm on the button with 3♥5♦. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play — calling requires ~40% equity to be immediately profitable, and 3-5 offsuit has far less than that multiway. Raising to try a steal is thin: the small blind (Flash) and big blind (Elon) have deep stacks and will often defend or 3‑bet, which would put me in tough spots with a very weak hand. With only a couple hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack (9550), preserving chips and waiting for a much better spot is +EV. Fold preflop.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the small blind, which is a premium hand in a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on Elon in the big blind, as my hand is significantly ahead of a random range.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind after Minni folded on the button, which is a standard steal attempt with a wide range given the position. Qd7s is a playable hand out of position heads-up: queen-high has showdown value, potential for top pair/top kicker, straight draws, and flush draw blocker. Pot odds are excellent (200 to call into 400, becoming 600), offering about 33% equity needed, and vs. a loose SB open (~40-50% range), Q7o has ~38-42% equity raw and plays well postflop. With deep stacks and tournament near end, defending wide maximizes EV rather than folding equity blindly. Plan: call, play postflop aggressively if hit, fold to c-bets if miss completely. No 3-bet value, fold too tight OOP.

I have flopped trips (Jacks) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a 6s-Jc-Jh board. This is an exceptionally strong hand. Since the board is paired and relatively dry, I want to extract value from Elon's range, which likely includes pocket pairs, 6x hands, or some Ace-high combinations that might peel one street. I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of about 40% of the pot to keep his range wide and induce calls or even a loose raise.

Facing a small flop c-bet of 250 into 850 from Flash's SB opening range on a paired J-J-6 board. Qd7s has queen-high, backdoor flush draw at best, no pair, no straight draw, and limited showdown value. While pot odds are ~3.4:1 (needing ~23% equity), Q7o has poor equity against Flash's likely range including Jx, sets, two pairs, and bluffs, but the paired board strengthens value hands and reduces bluff frequency. Pre-flop call was wide OOP defense, but this is a complete miss per plan: fold to c-bet, preserve stack with tournament nearing end (148/150), no need to speculate further with 10k stack deep vs Flash's 19k.

I am currently the chip leader by a significant margin as we approach the final two hands of the tournament. Being on the button in a three-handed game, I have a wide opening range. King-Deuce suited is a solid hand to open with in this position to put pressure on Elon and Minni, who are both sitting around 100 big blinds. A standard raise to 300 allows me to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has flush potential and top-pair strength.

Td4c is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no suits, and poor high card strength. In the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB), calling 250 more into a 450 pot offers terrible implied odds and equity against a wide button range. With 3 players left in a high-stakes tournament on hand 149/150, stack preservation is key. My 10k stack is playable, but speculative calls here risk unnecessary chips without strong post-flop potential. Opponents' stacks (Flash deep, Minni similar) don't change this; standard fold to avoid dominated spots.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with 7♣4♦. This is a very weak, offsuit hand — not a 3‑bet candidate against a deep, aggressive button. However the price to see a flop is small: I need to call 200 more (300 total) into a 450 pot, giving good pot odds to defend versus a wide opening range. I’ll call and play the flop straightforwardly — check and fold to large continuation bets when I miss, and only continue with a pair or a clear draw. Folding surrenders the pot but is too tight given the cheap price; raising would be unnecessary and risky.

Board: Ac Qc 4s — my hand 7c4d = a vulnerable middle pair (pair of fours). Flash was the preflop raiser so their range contains lots of hands that beat me (Ax, Qx, club draws, strong broadways). There’s no bet to call on the flop, so the best line is pot control: check and keep the pot small with a marginal holding, induce bluffs, and see how Flash proceeds. I’ll call small continuation bets to realize equity against bluffs/draws but fold to a sizable barrel that represents top pair or a strong club. A blocking bet here would be unnecessary and would commit chips OOP with a weak pair.

The flop of Ac-Qc-4s is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range. As the aggressor, I have all the strongest hands in my range (AA, QQ, AQ, AK), while the big blind's defending range consists more of middling cards and speculative hands. Even though I only hold King-high, I will fire a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to capitalize on this range advantage and take the pot down immediately. If Minni shows resistance by calling or raising, I can easily fold or check down the rest of the hand, as maintaining my chip lead for the final two hands is paramount.

Call 250. I have a marginal but real hand — middle pair (4s) with 7♣4♦ on Ac Qc 4♠. The pot is ~900, so calling 250 gives me ~3.6:1 (≈22% break‑even) — enough to continue vs a wide button c‑bet. Flash’s bet is small relative to the pot and consistent with a wide continuation range (Ax/Qx, club draws, broadway bluffs). Raising out of position with this weak pair is unnecessary and risky; folding surrenders too much equity for a cheap price. Plan after I call: keep the pot controlled — fold to a large, polarized barrel that clearly represents top pair/flush (e.g. pot‑sized or overbet) unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a strong draw; call smaller turn bets to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the river.

Check. I have a vulnerable single pair (4♦ with 7♣) on a dangerous three‑club board (A♣ Q♣ 4♠ 2♣). Flash opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range includes lots of A/Q hands, club combinations (now completed flushes if he held two clubs), and stronger made hands — plus some bluffs. Betting OOP here has very thin value (worse hands fold, better hands call) and needlessly bloats the pot when I’m likely behind. Pot control is the priority with a marginal holding: I’ll check, call small/medium bets that give me correct pot odds to realize equity, and fold to large, polarized barrels. If Flash checks back I go to the river and re‑evaluate.

I have improved to a pair of deuces, but the turn 2c is a dangerous card as it completes the club flush. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that there are only two hands left in the tournament, I should prioritize pot control and risk minimization. Checking behind allows me to see the river for free, avoids a potential check-raise, and keeps the pot manageable while I still have some showdown value.

Board: A♣ Q♣ 4♠ 2♣ J♠. I hold 7♣4♦ — one pair (fours) with a club blocker. Flash opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn after the second club and checked the river. His line is wide but contains many hands that beat me (A/Q/J combos, some two‑pair/sets, and any two‑club hands that make the flush). A river bet here has very thin value — it will fold out some air but will be called/raised by almost every hand that currently beats me, so it’s a losing line long‑term. With a marginal made hand and both players showing passive turn/river behavior, the correct play is to protect my stack and take the showdown: check and call only small thin bets, fold to large river aggression. Action: check.

I have a pair of deuces on a board with three clubs and several overcards (A, Q, J). While I may have the best hand if Minni was on a draw or holding a very weak pair, there is little value in betting as better hands won't fold and worse hands won't call. Given my massive chip lead and the fact that this is the penultimate hand of the tournament, I will check behind to realize my showdown value and avoid any unnecessary risk.

3-handed on the button with KTo, a strong hand in position. Pot is 150, facing a 100 to call (likely SB limped/completed). Raising takes initiative, builds the pot with position advantage, and KTs has good playability postflop. Min raise to 200 total is too small; standard raise to 3x the bet (300 total) pressures opponents and denies equity. Effective stacks are deep (~100BB), so no need to shove. Opponent tendencies from history show frequent calling but passive postflop checking.

Qs9c is a marginal, unsuited queen-handed and I’m out of position versus the button’s open. I won’t 3‑bet light here — the price to see a flop is cheap (250 more; 300 total) relative to my 10,100 stack, and Elon opens a wide range so I have reasonable equity to continue if I connect. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously OOP — fold to large turn/river pressure if I miss, but continue/aggress only with a top pair, two pair, a strong draw, or a favorable board texture. Calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack for better spots.

I am the current chip leader by a significant margin. As this is the final hand of the tournament, my priority is to preserve my stack and lock in the win. While 3d4d is suited, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise and a call. Folding ensures I maintain my lead without risking chips in a marginal situation where the expected value of playing is low compared to the risk of losing more chips.

I called preflop with Q♠9♣ intending to play cautiously out of position. On T♦ J♦ 5♣ I’ve missed entirely (no pair, no diamond draw) and Elon (the preflop raiser/button) will c‑bet this texture very frequently. With no showdown or fold equity worth risking a big pot OOP, I’ll control the pot and check. Plan: check and fold to any standard-sized continuation bet; only consider calling very tiny probes (~¼ pot or less) as a pure pot‑odds call, otherwise preserve my ~98bb stack for better spots.

Flop of Td Jd 5c is coordinated with diamond flush draw and straight draw potential (e.g., 89, Q9). Holding KTs offsuit (Tc Ks), I have king-high with a backdoor club flush draw and backdoor straight draw potential. Preflop I raised from button, representing a strong range, and Minni called from SB before checking, suggesting possible weakness or trapping. In position heads-up with deep stacks (~100BB), a continuation bet protects my hand, denies equity to draws, and takes advantage of fold equity against Minni's history of passive checking postflop. Betting ~40-50% pot balances value from Ax, overpairs, and thin value while protecting against draws. Pot is 700, so bet 350 to maintain sizing discipline and build pot if called by worse.

I checked OOP on T♦ J♦ 5♣ and Elon fired 350 into a 1050 pot (~1/3 pot). Calling 350 to win 1050 requires ~25% equity (350 / (1050+350) = 25%). With Q♠9♣ I have no pair, no diamond draw, only one overcard (Q) and very poor playability out of position. Elon is the preflop raiser/button and his c‑bet range here will include many T/J combos, A/Kx, and diamond draws that dominate or are ahead of me, so my realistic equity is well under 25%. There’s no fold equity or a reliable turn plan to justify investing more chips. Preserve ~98bb for better spots — fold.
